question_html:
1. Arrange the following in decreasing order of {tex}{pK}_{{b}}{/tex}:
    Aniline, p-nitroaniline, p-methylaniline
  2. Account for the following:
    1. Diazonium salts of aromatic amines are more stable than those of aliphatic amines.
    2. Methylamine in water reacts with {tex}{FeCl}_{3}{/tex} to precipitate hydrated ferric oxide.
question_text: Arrange the following in decreasing order of {tex}{pK}_{{b}}{/tex} : Aniline, p-nitroaniline, p-methylaniline Account for the following: Diazonium salts of aromatic amines are more stable than those of aliphatic amines. Methylamine in water reacts with {tex}{FeCl}_{3}{/tex} to precipitate hydrated ferric oxide.
explanation: p-nitroaniline > Aniline > p-methylaniline Due to resonance stabilization. Methylamine in water acts as a base and release {tex}{OH}^{-}{/tex} ions which reacts with {tex}{FeCl}_{3}{/tex} to form hydrated ferric oxide. /chemical equation
solution: p-nitroaniline > Aniline > p-methylaniline Due to resonance stabilization. Methylamine in water acts as a base and release {tex}{OH}^{-}{/tex} ions which reacts with {tex}{FeCl}_{3}{/tex} to form hydrated ferric oxide. /chemical equation
solution_html:
1. p-nitroaniline > Aniline > p-methylaniline
    1. Due to resonance stabilization.
    2. Methylamine in water acts as a base and release {tex}{OH}^{-}{/tex}ions which reacts with {tex}{FeCl}_{3}{/tex} to form hydrated ferric oxide. /chemical equation

question_html:
1. Arrange the following compounds in increasing order of their boiling point :
    {tex}\left({CH}_{3}\right)_{2} {NH}, {CH}_{3} {CH}_{2} {NH}_{2}, {CH}_{3} {CH}_{2} {OH}{/tex}.
  2. Give plausible explanation for each of the following:
    1. Aromatic primary amines cannot be prepared by Gabriel Phthalimide synthesis.
    2. Amides are less basic than amines.
question_text: Arrange the following compounds in increasing order of their boiling point : {tex}\left({CH}_{3}\right)_{2} {NH}, {CH}_{3} {CH}_{2} {NH}_{2}, {CH}_{3} {CH}_{2} {OH}{/tex} . Give plausible explanation for each of the following: Aromatic primary amines cannot be prepared by Gabriel Phthalimide synthesis. Amides are less basic than amines.
explanation: {tex}\left({CH}_{3}\right)_{2} {NH} \lt {CH}_{3} {CH}_{2} {NH}_{2} \lt {CH}_{3} {CH}_{2} {OH}{/tex} aromatic halides do not undergo nucleophilic substitution with the anion formed by phthalimide. /Due to resonance the lone pair on nitrogen is less available for donation/ Due to {tex}+R{/tex} effect lone pair of electrons is not easily available on N of - {tex}{NH}_{2}{/tex} group/ Due to -R effect of carbonyl group, electron density on N atom of {tex}{NH}_{2}{/tex} group decreases.
solution: {tex}\left({CH}_{3}\right)_{2} {NH} \lt {CH}_{3} {CH}_{2} {NH}_{2} \lt {CH}_{3} {CH}_{2} {OH}{/tex} aromatic halides do not undergo nucleophilic substitution with the anion formed by phthalimide. /Due to resonance the lone pair on nitrogen is less available for donation/ Due to {tex}+R{/tex} effect lone pair of electrons is not easily available on N of - {tex}{NH}_{2}{/tex} group/ Due to -R effect of carbonyl group, electron density on N atom of {tex}{NH}_{2}{/tex} group decreases.
solution_html:
1. {tex}\left({CH}_{3}\right)_{2} {NH} \lt {CH}_{3} {CH}_{2} {NH}_{2} \lt {CH}_{3} {CH}_{2} {OH}{/tex}
    1. aromatic halides do not undergo nucleophilic substitution with the anion formed by phthalimide.

    2. /Due to resonance the lone pair on nitrogen is less available for donation/ Due to {tex}+R{/tex} effect lone pair of electrons is not easily available on N of -{tex}{NH}_{2}{/tex} group/ Due to -R effect of carbonyl group, electron density on N atom of {tex}{NH}_{2}{/tex} group decreases.

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
Amines are usually formed from amides, imides, halides, nitro compounds, etc. They exhibit hydrogen bonding which influences their physical properties. In alkyl amines, a combination of electron releasing, steric and H-bonding factors influence the stability of the substituted ammonium cations in protic polar solvents and thus affect the basic nature of amines. Alkyl amines are found to be stronger bases than ammonia. Amines being basic in nature, react with acids to form salts. Aryldiazonium salts, undergo replacement of the diazonium group with a variety of nucleophiles to produce aryl halides, cyanides, phenols and arenes.
Answer the following questions:

  1. How can you convert the following? (2)
    1. Ethanoic acid to methanamine
    2. Propanenitrile to 1-aminopropane
  2. Why is {tex}{pK}_{{b}}{/tex} value of aniline more than that of methylamine? (1)
    1. Arrange the following in increasing order of their basic strength in aqueous solution: (1){tex}{CH}_{3}-{NH}_{2}, \left({CH}_{3}\right)_{2} {NH}, \left({CH}_{3}\right)_{3} {~N}{/tex}

      OR

    2. Give the structures of A and B in the following reaction: (1)
      {tex}{C}_{6} {H}_{5} {NO}_{2} \xrightarrow{{Fe} / {HCl}} {~A} \xrightarrow[273 {~K}]{{HNO}_{2}} {~B}{/tex}
question_text: Amines are usually formed from amides, imides, halides, nitro compounds, etc. They exhibit hydrogen bonding which influences their physical properties. In alkyl amines, a combination of electron releasing, steric and H-bonding factors influence the stability of the substituted ammonium cations in protic polar solvents and thus affect the basic nature of amines. Alkyl amines are found to be stronger bases than ammonia. Amines being basic in nature, react with acids to form salts. Aryldiazonium salts, undergo replacement of the diazonium group with a variety of nucleophiles to produce aryl halides, cyanides, phenols and arenes. Answer the following questions: How can you convert the following? (2) Ethanoic acid to methanamine Propanenitrile to 1-aminopropane Why is {tex}{pK}_{{b}}{/tex} value of aniline more than that of methylamine? (1) Arrange the following in increasing order of their basic strength in aqueous solution: (1) {tex}{CH}_{3}-{NH}_{2}, \left({CH}_{3}\right)_{2} {NH}, \left({CH}_{3}\right)_{3} {~N}{/tex} OR Give the structures of A and B in the following reaction: (1) {tex}{C}_{6} {H}_{5} {NO}_{2} \xrightarrow{{Fe} / {HCl}} {~A} \xrightarrow[273 {~K}]{{HNO}_{2}} {~B}{/tex}
explanation: Aniline undergoes resonance and as a result the electrons on the N -atom are less available for donation. {tex}\left({CH}_{3}\right)_{3} {~N} \lt {CH}_{3} {NH}_{2} \lt \left({CH}_{3}\right)_{2} {NH}{/tex} OR {tex}{A}={C}_{6} {H}_{5} {NH}_{2} ; {B}=={C}_{6} {H}_{5} {~N}_{2}{ }^{+} {Cl}{/tex}
solution: Aniline undergoes resonance and as a result the electrons on the N -atom are less available for donation. {tex}\left({CH}_{3}\right)_{3} {~N} \lt {CH}_{3} {NH}_{2} \lt \left({CH}_{3}\right)_{2} {NH}{/tex} OR {tex}{A}={C}_{6} {H}_{5} {NH}_{2} ; {B}=={C}_{6} {H}_{5} {~N}_{2}{ }^{+} {Cl}{/tex}
solution_html:
2. Aniline undergoes resonance and as a result the electrons on the N -atom are less available for donation.
    1. {tex}\left({CH}_{3}\right)_{3} {~N} \lt {CH}_{3} {NH}_{2} \lt \left({CH}_{3}\right)_{2} {NH}{/tex}

      OR

    2. {tex}{A}={C}_{6} {H}_{5} {NH}_{2} ; {B}=={C}_{6} {H}_{5} {~N}_{2}{ }^{+} {Cl}{/tex}

1. In a chemistry practical class, the teacher gave his students an amine 'X' having molecular formula {tex}{C}_{2} {H}_{7} {~N}{/tex}, and asked the students to identify the type of amine. One of the students, Neeta, observed that it reacts with {tex}{C}_{6} {H}_{5} {SO}_{2} {Cl}{/tex}, to give a compound which dissolves in NaOH solution. Can you help Neeta to identify the compound 'X'?
  2. Arrange the following in the increasing order of their {tex}{pK}_{{b}}{/tex} value in aqueous phase:
    {tex}{C}_{6} {H}_{5} {NH}_{2},\left({CH}_{3}\right)_{2} {NH}, {/tex}{tex}{NH}_{3}, {CH}_{3} {NH}_{2},\left({CH}_{3}\right)_{3} {~N}{/tex}
  3. Aniline on nitration gives considerable amount of meta product along with ortho and para products. Why?
  4. Convert aniline to:
    1. p-bromoaniline
    2. phenol
question_text: In a chemistry practical class, the teacher gave his students an amine 'X' having molecular formula {tex}{C}_{2} {H}_{7} {~N}{/tex} , and asked the students to identify the type of amine. One of the students, Neeta, observed that it reacts with {tex}{C}_{6} {H}_{5} {SO}_{2} {Cl}{/tex} , to give a compound which dissolves in NaOH solution. Can you help Neeta to identify the compound 'X'? Arrange the following in the increasing order of their {tex}{pK}_{{b}}{/tex} value in aqueous phase: {tex}{C}_{6} {H}_{5} {NH}_{2},\left({CH}_{3}\right)_{2} {NH}, {/tex} {tex}{NH}_{3}, {CH}_{3} {NH}_{2},\left({CH}_{3}\right)_{3} {~N}{/tex} Aniline on nitration gives considerable amount of meta product along with ortho and para products. Why? Convert aniline to: p-bromoaniline phenol
explanation: Amine ' {tex}X{/tex} ' react with {tex}{C}_{6} {H}_{5} {SO}_{2} {Cl}{/tex} to give a compound, soluble in NaOH so amine ' {tex}X{/tex} ' is primary amine, {tex}{CH}_{3} {CH}_{2} {NH}_{2}{/tex} /Ethanamine/Ethyl amine {tex}\left({CH}_{3}\right)_{2} {NH} \lt {CH}_{3} {NH}_{2} \lt {/tex} {tex}\left({CH}_{3}\right)_{3} {~N} \lt {NH}_{3} \lt {C}_{6} {H}_{5} {NH}_{2}{/tex} In the strongly acidic medium, aniline is protonated to anilinium ion, which is metadirecting. {tex} {C}_6 {H}_5 {NH}_2+{NaNO}_2+2 {HCl} \stackrel{\left(0-5^{\circ} {C}\right)}{\longrightarrow} {/tex} {tex}{C}_6 {H}_5 {~N}_2+{Cl}^{-} \xrightarrow{{H}_2 {O}, 283 {~K}} {C}_6 {H}_5 {OH} {/tex}
solution: Amine ' {tex}X{/tex} ' react with {tex}{C}_{6} {H}_{5} {SO}_{2} {Cl}{/tex} to give a compound, soluble in NaOH so amine ' {tex}X{/tex} ' is primary amine, {tex}{CH}_{3} {CH}_{2} {NH}_{2}{/tex} /Ethanamine/Ethyl amine {tex}\left({CH}_{3}\right)_{2} {NH} \lt {CH}_{3} {NH}_{2} \lt {/tex} {tex}\left({CH}_{3}\right)_{3} {~N} \lt {NH}_{3} \lt {C}_{6} {H}_{5} {NH}_{2}{/tex} In the strongly acidic medium, aniline is protonated to anilinium ion, which is metadirecting. {tex} {C}_6 {H}_5 {NH}_2+{NaNO}_2+2 {HCl} \stackrel{\left(0-5^{\circ} {C}\right)}{\longrightarrow} {/tex} {tex}{C}_6 {H}_5 {~N}_2+{Cl}^{-} \xrightarrow{{H}_2 {O}, 283 {~K}} {C}_6 {H}_5 {OH} {/tex}
solution_html:
1. Amine '{tex}X{/tex}' react with {tex}{C}_{6} {H}_{5} {SO}_{2} {Cl}{/tex} to give a compound, soluble in NaOH so amine '{tex}X{/tex}' is primary amine, {tex}{CH}_{3} {CH}_{2} {NH}_{2}{/tex} /Ethanamine/Ethyl amine
  2. {tex}\left({CH}_{3}\right)_{2} {NH} \lt {CH}_{3} {NH}_{2} \lt {/tex}{tex}\left({CH}_{3}\right)_{3} {~N} \lt {NH}_{3} \lt {C}_{6} {H}_{5} {NH}_{2}{/tex}
  3. In the strongly acidic medium, aniline is protonated to anilinium ion, which is metadirecting.
    2. {tex} {C}_6 {H}_5 {NH}_2+{NaNO}_2+2 {HCl} \stackrel{\left(0-5^{\circ} {C}\right)}{\longrightarrow} {/tex}{tex}{C}_6 {H}_5 {~N}_2+{Cl}^{-} \xrightarrow{{H}_2 {O}, 283 {~K}} {C}_6 {H}_5 {OH} {/tex}

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
Amines have a lone pair of electrons on nitrogen atom due to which they behave as Lewis base. Greater the value of {tex}{K}_{{b}}{/tex} or smaller the value of {tex}{pK}_{{b}}{/tex}, stronger is the base. Amines are more basic than alcohols, ethers, esters, etc. The basic character of aliphatic amines should increase with the increase of alkyl substitution. But it does not occur in a regular manner as a secondary aliphatic amine is unexpectedly more basic than a tertiary amine in aqueous solutions. Aromatic amines are weaker bases than ammonia and aliphatic amines. Electron releasing groups such as {tex}-{CH}_{3}{/tex}, {tex}-{OCH}_{3},-{NH}_{2}{/tex}, etc., increase the basicity while electron-withdrawing substituents such as {tex}-{NO}_{2},-{CN}{/tex}, halogens etc., decrease the basicity of amines. The effect of these substitute is more at {tex}{p}^{-}{/tex}than at {tex}{m}^{-}{/tex}position.

  1. Arrange the following in the increasing order of their basic character.
    Give reason: (2)
  2.  Why {tex}{pK}_{{b}}{/tex} of aniline is more than that of methylamine? (1)
    1. Arrange the following in the increasing order of their basic character in an aqueous solution: (1){tex}\left({CH}_{3}\right)_{3} {~N},\left({CH}_{3}\right)_{2} {NH}, {NH}_{3}, {CH}_{3} {NH}_{2}{/tex}

      OR

    2. Why ammonolysis of alkyl halides is not a good method to prepare pure amines? (1)
question_text: Amines have a lone pair of electrons on nitrogen atom due to which they behave as Lewis base. Greater the value of {tex}{K}_{{b}}{/tex} or smaller the value of {tex}{pK}_{{b}}{/tex} , stronger is the base. Amines are more basic than alcohols, ethers, esters, etc. The basic character of aliphatic amines should increase with the increase of alkyl substitution. But it does not occur in a regular manner as a secondary aliphatic amine is unexpectedly more basic than a tertiary amine in aqueous solutions. Aromatic amines are weaker bases than ammonia and aliphatic amines. Electron releasing groups such as {tex}-{CH}_{3}{/tex} , {tex}-{OCH}_{3},-{NH}_{2}{/tex} , etc., increase the basicity while electron-withdrawing substituents such as {tex}-{NO}_{2},-{CN}{/tex} , halogens etc., decrease the basicity of amines. subject: Chemistry
question_html:
Amines are usually formed from nitro compounds, halides, amides, imides, etc. They exhibit hydrogen bonding which influences their physical properties. In alkyl amines, a combination of electron releasing, steric and hydrogen bonding factors influence the stability of the substituted ammonium cations in protic polar solvents and thus affect the basic nature of amines. In aromatic amines, electron releasing and withdrawing groups, respectively increase and decrease their basic character. Influence of the number of hydrogen atoms at nitrogen atom on the type of reactions and nature of products is responsible for identification and distinction between primary, secondary and tertiary amines. Presence of amino group in aromatic ring enhances reactivity of the aromatic amines. Aryl diazonium salts provide advantageous methods for producing aryl halides, cyanides, phenols and arenes by reductive removal of the diazo group.

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
1. Aromatic primary amines cannot be prepared by Gabriel Phthalimide synthesis. Why?
  2. Aniline is a weaker base than alkyl amines. Why?
  3. Arrange the following in increasing order of basic strength in aqueous solution:
    (CH3CH2)2NH, (C2H5)3N, C2H5NH2
question_text: Aromatic primary amines cannot be prepared by Gabriel Phthalimide synthesis. Why? Aniline is a weaker base than alkyl amines. Why? Arrange the following in increasing order of basic strength in aqueous solution: (CH 3 CH 2 ) 2 NH, (C 2 H 5 ) 3 N, C 2 H 5 NH 2
explanation: Aromatic primary amines cannot be prepared by Gabriel phthalimide synthesis as aryl halides do not undergo nucleophilic substitution with the salt formed by phthalimide. Lone pair on Nitrogen in aniline are involved in resonance with the benzene ring and therefore not available for donation to that extent as in NH 3 According to Lewis theory, the species which easily loses a pair of electron is Lewis base, Since, ammonia can lose easily electron pair while aniline cannot, thus ammonia is a stronger base than aniline. +I effect of alkyl group increases electron density on −N of amine and increases basic strength. Thus (C 2 H 5 ) 2 NH is strongest base. In aniline, the lone pair of electrons of N is in delocalization with ring which makes it less available and least basic. Two Methyl substitutions increases basic strength due to its inductive effect but the steric effect also influences it.
solution: Aromatic primary amines cannot be prepared by Gabriel phthalimide synthesis as aryl halides do not undergo nucleophilic substitution with the salt formed by phthalimide. Lone pair on Nitrogen in aniline are involved in resonance with the benzene ring and therefore not available for donation to that extent as in NH 3 According to Lewis theory, the species which easily loses a pair of electron is Lewis base, Since, ammonia can lose easily electron pair while aniline cannot, thus ammonia is a stronger base than aniline. +I effect of alkyl group increases electron density on −N of amine and increases basic strength. Thus (C 2 H 5 ) 2 NH is strongest base. In aniline, the lone pair of electrons of N is in delocalization with ring which makes it less available and least basic. Two Methyl substitutions increases basic strength due to its inductive effect but the steric effect also influences it.
solution_html:
1. Aromatic primary amines cannot be prepared by Gabriel phthalimide synthesis as aryl halides do not undergo nucleophilic substitution with the salt formed by phthalimide.
  2. Lone pair on Nitrogen in aniline are involved in resonance with the benzene ring and therefore not available for donation to that extent as in NH3 According to Lewis theory, the species which easily loses a pair of electron is Lewis base, Since, ammonia can lose easily electron pair while aniline cannot, thus ammonia is a stronger base than aniline.
  3. +I effect of alkyl group increases electron density on −N of amine and increases basic strength. Thus (C2H5)2NH is strongest base. In aniline, the lone pair of electrons of N is in delocalization with ring which makes it less available and least basic. Two Methyl substitutions increases basic strength due to its inductive effect but the steric effect also influences it.

question_text: Choose the primary amine from the following compounds: (C 2 H 5 ) 2 CHNH 2 , (C 2 H 5 ) 2 NH, (CH 3 ) 3 N
explanation: The primary amine among the given compounds is (C 2 H5) 2 CHNH 2 . In this compound, (C 2 H 5 ) 2 CHNH 2 , you can see that the nitrogen atom (N) is bonded to two hydrogen atoms (H) and one carbon atom (C), which is the defining characteristic of a primary amine. Primary amines have the general structure R-NH 2 , where R represents an alkyl or aryl group. In this case, (C 2 H 5 ) 2 CH represents the alkyl group, and NH 2 represents the amino group bonded to the primary carbon.

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
Illustrate the following reactions giving suitable example in each case:

  1. Gabriel phthalimide synthesis.
  2. Carbylamine reaction.
  3. Hoffmann bromamide degradation reaction.
question_text: Illustrate the following reactions giving suitable example in each case: Gabriel phthalimide synthesis. Carbylamine reaction. Hoffmann bromamide degradation reaction.
explanation: Gabriel phthalimide synthesis: is used for the preparation of aliphatic primary amines. In this reaction, phthalimide is first of all treated with ethanolic KOH to form potassium phthalimide. Potassium phthalimide on treatment with alkyl halide gives N-alkyl phthalimide, which on hydrolysis with dilute hydrochloric acid gives a primary amine as the product. Carbylamine reaction: is used as a test for the identification of primary amines. When aliphatic and aromatic primary amines are heated with chloroform and ethanolic potassium hydroxide, carbylamines (or isocyanides) are formed. These carbylamines have very unpleasant odours. Secondary and tertiary amines do not respond to this test. {tex}\underset{Primary\;amine}{R-NH_2}\;{/tex} + {tex}\;\underset{Chloroform}{CHCL_3}\;{/tex} + {tex}\;\underset{hydroxide}{\underset{Potassium}{3KOH(alc.)}}\;{/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow\triangle\;{/tex} {tex}\underset{Carbylamine}{R-NC}{/tex} + {tex}\;3KCL\;{/tex} + {tex}\;3H_2O{/tex} For example, {tex}\underset{Methnamine}{CH_3-NH_2}\;{/tex} + {tex}\;\underset{Chloroform}{CHCL_3}\;{/tex} + {tex}\;\underset{hydroxide}{\underset{Potassium}{3KOH(alc.)}}\;{/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow\triangle\;{/tex} {tex}\underset{or\;methyl\;isocyanide}{\underset{Methyl\;Carbylamine}{CH_3-NC}}\;{/tex} + {tex}\;3KCL\;{/tex} + {tex}\;3H_2O{/tex} Hoffmann bromamide reaction: When an amide is treated with bromine in an aqueous or ethanolic solution of sodium hydroxide, a primary amine with one carbon atom less than the original amide is produced. This degradation reaction is known as Hoffmann bromamide reaction. This reaction involves the migration of an alkyl or aryl group from the carbonyl carbon atom of the amide to the nitrogen atom. For example,
solution: Gabriel phthalimide synthesis: is used for the preparation of aliphatic primary amines. In this reaction, phthalimide is first of all treated with ethanolic KOH to form potassium phthalimide. Potassium phthalimide on treatment with alkyl halide gives N-alkyl phthalimide, which on hydrolysis with dilute hydrochloric acid gives a primary amine as the product. Carbylamine reaction: is used as a test for the identification of primary amines. When aliphatic and aromatic primary amines are heated with chloroform and ethanolic potassium hydroxide, carbylamines (or isocyanides) are formed. These carbylamines have very unpleasant odours. Secondary and tertiary amines do not respond to this test. {tex}\underset{Primary\;amine}{R-NH_2}\;{/tex} + {tex}\;\underset{Chloroform}{CHCL_3}\;{/tex} + {tex}\;\underset{hydroxide}{\underset{Potassium}{3KOH(alc.)}}\;{/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow\triangle\;{/tex} {tex}\underset{Carbylamine}{R-NC}{/tex} + {tex}\;3KCL\;{/tex} + {tex}\;3H_2O{/tex} For example, {tex}\underset{Methnamine}{CH_3-NH_2}\;{/tex} + {tex}\;\underset{Chloroform}{CHCL_3}\;{/tex} + {tex}\;\underset{hydroxide}{\underset{Potassium}{3KOH(alc.)}}\;{/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow\triangle\;{/tex} {tex}\underset{or\;methyl\;isocyanide}{\underset{Methyl\;Carbylamine}{CH_3-NC}}\;{/tex} + {tex}\;3KCL\;{/tex} + {tex}\;3H_2O{/tex} Hoffmann bromamide reaction: When an amide is treated with bromine in an aqueous or ethanolic solution of sodium hydroxide, a primary amine with one carbon atom less than the original amide is produced. This degradation reaction is known as Hoffmann bromamide reaction. This reaction involves the migration of an alkyl or aryl group from the carbonyl carbon atom of the amide to the nitrogen atom. For example,
solution_html:
1. Gabriel phthalimide synthesis: is used for the preparation of aliphatic primary amines. In this reaction, phthalimide is first of all treated with ethanolic KOH to form potassium phthalimide. Potassium phthalimide on treatment with alkyl halide gives N-alkyl phthalimide, which on hydrolysis with dilute hydrochloric acid gives a primary amine as the product.
  2. Carbylamine reaction: is used as a test for the identification of primary amines. When aliphatic and aromatic primary amines are heated with chloroform and ethanolic potassium hydroxide, carbylamines (or isocyanides) are formed. These carbylamines have very unpleasant odours. Secondary and tertiary amines do not respond to this test.
    {tex}\underset{Primary\;amine}{R-NH_2}\;{/tex}+ {tex}\;\underset{Chloroform}{CHCL_3}\;{/tex} + {tex}\;\underset{hydroxide}{\underset{Potassium}{3KOH(alc.)}}\;{/tex}{tex}\xrightarrow\triangle\;{/tex}{tex}\underset{Carbylamine}{R-NC}{/tex} + {tex}\;3KCL\;{/tex}+ {tex}\;3H_2O{/tex}
    For example,
    {tex}\underset{Methnamine}{CH_3-NH_2}\;{/tex}+ {tex}\;\underset{Chloroform}{CHCL_3}\;{/tex}+ {tex}\;\underset{hydroxide}{\underset{Potassium}{3KOH(alc.)}}\;{/tex}{tex}\xrightarrow\triangle\;{/tex}{tex}\underset{or\;methyl\;isocyanide}{\underset{Methyl\;Carbylamine}{CH_3-NC}}\;{/tex}+ {tex}\;3KCL\;{/tex}+ {tex}\;3H_2O{/tex}
  3. Hoffmann bromamide reaction:
    When an amide is treated with bromine in an aqueous or ethanolic solution of sodium hydroxide, a primary amine with one carbon atom less than the original amide is produced. subject: Chemistry
question_html:
Write reasons for the following:

  1. Ethylamine is soluble in water whereas aniline is insoluble.
  2. Amino group is o- and p-directing in aromatic electrophilic substitution reactions, but aniline on nitration gives a substantial amount of m-nitroaniline.
  3. Amines behave as nucleophiles.
question_text: Write reasons for the following: Ethylamine is soluble in water whereas aniline is insoluble. Amino group is o- and p-directing in aromatic electrophilic substitution reactions, but aniline on nitration gives a substantial amount of m-nitroaniline. Amines behave as nucleophiles.
explanation: Ethylamine forms strong hydrogen bonds with water molecules whereas in aniline due to the large hydrocarbon part, the extent of H-bonding decreases. Because of protonation of aniline / formation of anilinium ion which deactivates the ring. Amines behaves as nucleophiles due to the presence of a lone pair of electrons on the nitrogen atom.
solution: Ethylamine forms strong hydrogen bonds with water molecules whereas in aniline due to the large hydrocarbon part, the extent of H-bonding decreases. Because of protonation of aniline / formation of anilinium ion which deactivates the ring. Amines behaves as nucleophiles due to the presence of a lone pair of electrons on the nitrogen atom.
solution_html:
1. Ethylamine forms strong hydrogen bonds with water molecules whereas in aniline due to the large hydrocarbon part, the extent of H-bonding decreases.
  2. Because of protonation of aniline / formation of anilinium ion which deactivates the ring.
  3. Amines behaves as nucleophiles due to the presence of a lone pair of electrons on the nitrogen atom.

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
1. Arrange the following compounds in the increasing order of their basic strength in aqueous solution:
    CH3 NH2, (CH3)3 N, (CH3)2 NH
  2. What is Hinsberg's reagent?
  3. What is the role of pyridine in the acylation reaction of amines?
question_text: Arrange the following compounds in the increasing order of their basic strength in aqueous solution: CH 3 NH 2 , (CH 3 ) 3 N, (CH 3 ) 2 NH What is Hinsberg's reagent? What is the role of pyridine in the acylation reaction of amines?
explanation: The increasing order of basic strength in aqueous solution of the following amines is (CH 3 ) 3 N < CH 3 NH 2 < (CH 3 ) 2 NH (Due to steric hindrance and +I effect of alky groups). Hinsberg reagent is also known as benzene sulphonyl chloride (C 6 H 6 SO 2 Cl). When it reacts with primary and secondary amines, it produces sulphonamides. Pyridine is used in the acylation of amines because it acts as a strong base which helps in removing the side product HCl from the reaction mixture. Therefore, it acts as an acceptor for the acid by-product formed during the reaction.
solution: The increasing order of basic strength in aqueous solution of the following amines is (CH 3 ) 3 N < CH 3 NH 2 < (CH 3 ) 2 NH (Due to steric hindrance and +I effect of alky groups). Hinsberg reagent is also known as benzene sulphonyl chloride (C 6 H 6 SO 2 Cl). When it reacts with primary and secondary amines, it produces sulphonamides. Pyridine is used in the acylation of amines because it acts as a strong base which helps in removing the side product HCl from the reaction mixture. Therefore, it acts as an acceptor for the acid by-product formed during the reaction.
solution_html:
1. The increasing order of basic strength in aqueous solution of the following amines is (CH3)3N < CH3NH2 < (CH3)2NH (Due to steric hindrance and +I effect of alky groups).
  2. Hinsberg reagent is also known as benzene sulphonyl chloride (C6H6SO2Cl). When it reacts with primary and secondary amines, it produces sulphonamides.
  3. Pyridine is used in the acylation of amines because it acts as a strong base which helps in removing the side product HCl from the reaction mixture. Therefore, it acts as an acceptor for the acid by-product formed during the reaction.

question_text: An aromatic compound A (C 7 H 6 O 2 ) on reaction with aqueous ammonia and heating forms compound B . B on heating with Br 2 and alcoholic potash forms a compound C of molecular formula C 6 H 7 N. Write the reactions involved and identify A , B , C .
explanation: The aromatic compound A is benzoic acid C 6 H 5 COOH. On treatment with aqueous ammonia and heating forms compound B, which is benzamide C 6 H 5 CONH 2 . Benzamide on heating with bromine and KOH forms a compound C of molecular formula C 6 H 7 N, which is aniline C 6 H 5 NH 2 . The reaction is called Hoffmann bromamide degradation.
solution: The aromatic compound A is benzoic acid C 6 H 5 COOH. On treatment with aqueous ammonia and heating forms compound B, which is benzamide C 6 H 5 CONH 2 . Benzamide on heating with bromine and KOH forms a compound C of molecular formula C 6 H 7 N, which is aniline C 6 H 5 NH 2 . The reaction is called Hoffmann bromamide degradation.
solution_html:
The aromatic compound A is benzoic acid C6H5COOH.
On treatment with aqueous ammonia and heating forms compound B, which is benzamide C6H5CONH2.
Benzamide on heating with bromine and KOH forms a compound C of molecular formula C6H7N, which is aniline C6H5NH2. The reaction is called Hoffmann bromamide degradation.

question_text: Account for the following: pk b of aniline is more than that of methylamine. Aniline does not undergo Friedel-Crafts reaction. Primary amines have higher boiling points than tertiary amines.
explanation: lone pair of electrons are delocalised over benzene ring due to resonance, electron density decreases and hence less available for protonation. Aniline is a Lewis base and it reacts with AlCl 3 to form a salt. Presence of intermolecular hydrogen bonding in primary amines which is absent in tertiary amines.
solution: lone pair of electrons are delocalised over benzene ring due to resonance, electron density decreases and hence less available for protonation. solution_html:
1. lone pair of electrons are delocalised over benzene ring due to resonance, electron density decreases and hence less available for protonation. 
  2. Aniline is a Lewis base and it reacts with AlCl3 to form a salt. 
  3. Presence of intermolecular hydrogen bonding in primary amines which is absent in tertiary amines.

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
Amines constitute an important class of organic compounds derived by replacing one or more hydrogen atoms of ammonia molecule by alkyl/aryl groups. Amines are usually formed from nitro compounds, halides, amides, etc. They exhibit hydrogen bonding which influences their physical properties. Alkyl amines are found to be stronger bases than ammonia. In aromatic amines, electron releasing and withdrawing groups, respectively increase and decrease their basic character. Reactions of amines are governed by availability of the unshared pair of electrons on nitrogen. Influence of the number of hydrogen atoms at nitrogen atom on the type of reactions and nature of products is responsible for identification and distinction between primary, secondary and tertiary amines. Reactivity of aromatic amines can be controlled by acylation process.


Why does aniline not give Friedel-Crafts reaction?
question_text: Amines constitute an important class of organic compounds derived by replacing one or more hydrogen atoms of ammonia molecule by alkyl/aryl groups. Amines are usually formed from nitro compounds, halides, amides, etc. They exhibit hydrogen bonding which influences their physical properties. Alkyl amines are found to be stronger bases than ammonia. In aromatic amines, electron releasing and withdrawing groups, respectively increase and decrease their basic character. Reactions of amines are governed by availability of the unshared pair of electrons on nitrogen. Influence of the number of hydrogen atoms at nitrogen atom on the type of reactions and nature of products is responsible for identification and distinction between primary, secondary and tertiary amines. Reactivity of aromatic amines can be controlled by acylation process. Why does aniline not give Friedel-Crafts reaction?
explanation: Aniline is a Lewis base and it reacts with AlCl 3 to form a salt / N of aniline acquires positive charge with AlCl 3 and hence is a deactivating group.
solution: Aniline is a Lewis base and it reacts with AlCl 3 to form a salt / N of aniline acquires positive charge with AlCl 3 and hence is a deactivating group.
solution_html: Aniline is a Lewis base and it reacts with AlCl3 to form a salt / N of aniline acquires positive charge with AlCl3 and hence is a deactivating group.

Hinsberg test is used for the identification and distinction between primary, secondary and tertiary amines. Which of the following reagents is used for the Hinsberg test of amines?
explanation: C 6 H 5 COCl

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
Account for the following:

  1. Aniline is a weaker base compared to ethanamine.
  2. Aniline does not undergo Friedel-Crafts reaction.
  3. Only aliphatic primary amines can be prepared by Gabriel Phthalimide synthesis.
question_text: Account for the following: Aniline is a weaker base compared to ethanamine. Aniline does not undergo Friedel-Crafts reaction. Only aliphatic primary amines can be prepared by Gabriel Phthalimide synthesis.
explanation: Aniline is typical of aromatic primary amines - where the -NH 2 group is attached directly to a benzene ring. These are very much weaker bases than ammonia. Aniline is more basic than ethylamine because of resonance. When aniline loses a proton the resulting ion is more stable than that of ethylamine and hence, aniline is more basic than ethylamine. Hence, aniline looses proton more readily than ethylamine. Aniline being a Lewis base reacts with Lewis acid (AlCl 3 ) to form a salt. {tex}\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{NH}_{2}+\mathrm{AlCl}_{3}{/tex} {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} {tex}{C_6}{H_5}\mathop N\limits^+{H_2}AlCl_3^ - {/tex} As a result, N acquires a positive charge so, it acts as a strong deactivating group for electrophilic substitution reaction. Thus, aniline does not undergo Friedel-Crafts reaction. Gabriel phthalimide synthesis is a very convenient method for the preparation of pure aliphatic amines Step 1: Phthalimide is treated with KOH to form potassium phthalimide Step 2: Potassium phthalimide is treated with a suitable alkyl halide to form N-substituted phthalimides. Step 3: N-substituted phthalimides undergoes hydrolysis in the presence of dil. HCl or with alkali(NaOH) to give primary amines. Overall reaction: Gabriel phthalimide synthesis results in the formation of primary(1° amine) only. Secondary or tertiary amines are not formed through this synthesis. Hence, Gabriel phthalimide synthesis preferred for the formation of primary amines only.
solution: Aniline is typical of aromatic primary amines - where the -NH 2 group is attached directly to a benzene ring. These are very much weaker bases than ammonia. Aniline is more basic than ethylamine because of resonance. When aniline loses a proton the resulting ion is more stable than that of ethylamine and hence, aniline is more basic than ethylamine. Hence, aniline looses proton more readily than ethylamine. Aniline being a Lewis base reacts with Lewis acid (AlCl 3 ) to form a salt. {tex}\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{NH}_{2}+\mathrm{AlCl}_{3}{/tex} {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} {tex}{C_6}{H_5}\mathop N\limits^+{H_2}AlCl_3^ - {/tex} As a result, N acquires a positive charge so, it acts as a strong deactivating group for electrophilic substitution reaction. Thus, aniline does not undergo Friedel-Crafts reaction. Gabriel phthalimide synthesis is a very convenient method for the preparation of pure aliphatic amines Step 1: Phthalimide is treated with KOH to form potassium phthalimide Step 2: Potassium phthalimide is treated with a suitable alkyl halide to form N-substituted phthalimides. Step 3: N-substituted phthalimides undergoes hydrolysis in the presence of dil. HCl or with alkali(NaOH) to give primary amines. Overall reaction: Gabriel phthalimide synthesis results in the formation of primary(1° amine) only. Secondary or tertiary amines are not formed through this synthesis. Hence, Gabriel phthalimide synthesis preferred for the formation of primary amines only.
solution_html:
1. Aniline is typical of aromatic primary amines - where the -NH2 group is attached directly to a benzene ring. These are very much weaker bases than ammonia.
    Aniline is more basic than ethylamine because of resonance. When aniline loses a proton the resulting ion is more stable than that of ethylamine and hence, aniline is more basic than ethylamine. Hence, aniline looses proton more readily than ethylamine.
  2. Aniline being a Lewis base reacts with Lewis acid (AlCl3) to form a salt.
    {tex}\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{NH}_{2}+\mathrm{AlCl}_{3}{/tex} {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} {tex}{C_6}{H_5}\mathop N\limits^+{H_2}AlCl_3^ - {/tex}
    As a result, N acquires a positive charge so, it acts as a strong deactivating group for electrophilic substitution reaction. Thus, aniline does not undergo Friedel-Crafts reaction.
  3. Gabriel phthalimide synthesis is a very convenient method for the preparation of pure aliphatic amines
    Step 1: Phthalimide is treated with KOH to form potassium phthalimide

    Step 2: Potassium phthalimide is treated with a suitable alkyl halide to form N-substituted phthalimides.

    Step 3: N-substituted phthalimides undergoes hydrolysis in the presence of dil. HCl or with alkali(NaOH) to give primary amines.

    Overall reaction:

    Gabriel phthalimide synthesis results in the formation of primary(1° amine) only. Secondary or tertiary amines are not formed through this synthesis. Hence, Gabriel phthalimide synthesis preferred for the formation of primary amines only.

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
Give reasons:

  1. Aniline does not undergo Friedal-Crafts reaction.
  2. Aromatic primary amines cannot be prepared by Gabriel’s phthalimide synthesis.
  3. Aliphatic amines are stronger bases than ammonia.
question_text: Give reasons: Aniline does not undergo Friedal-Crafts reaction. Aromatic primary amines cannot be prepared by Gabriel’s phthalimide synthesis. Aliphatic amines are stronger bases than ammonia.
explanation: Aniline being a Lewis base reacts with Lewis acid (AlCl 3 ) to form a salt. {tex}\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{NH}_{2}+\mathrm{AlCl}_{3}{/tex} {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} {tex}{C_6}{H_5}\mathop N\limits^+{H_2}AlCl_3^ - {/tex} As a result, N acquires a positive charge so, it acts as a strong deactivating group for electrophilic substitution reaction. Thus, aniline does not undergo Friedel-Crafts reaction. Primary aromatic amines cannot be prepared by Gabriel phthalimide synthesis because aryl halides do not undergo nucleophilic substitution with the anion formed by phthalimide. Gabriel phthalimide synthesis is used for the preparation of primary amines. Aliphatic amines are stronger bases than the ammonia because the alkyl group in aliphatic amines has +I effect. So the alkyl group tends to increase the electron density on the nitrogen atom whereas the electron releasing tendency of amines becomes more than that of ammonia.
solution: Aniline being a Lewis base reacts with Lewis acid (AlCl 3 ) to form a salt. {tex}\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{NH}_{2}+\mathrm{AlCl}_{3}{/tex} {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} {tex}{C_6}{H_5}\mathop N\limits^+{H_2}AlCl_3^ - {/tex} As a result, N acquires a positive charge so, it acts as a strong deactivating group for electrophilic substitution reaction. Thus, aniline does not undergo Friedel-Crafts reaction. Primary aromatic amines cannot be prepared by Gabriel phthalimide synthesis because aryl halides do not undergo nucleophilic substitution with the anion formed by phthalimide. Gabriel phthalimide synthesis is used for the preparation of primary amines. Aliphatic amines are stronger bases than the ammonia because the alkyl group in aliphatic amines has +I effect. So the alkyl group tends to increase the electron density on the nitrogen atom whereas the electron releasing tendency of amines becomes more than that of ammonia.
solution_html:
1. Aniline being a Lewis base reacts with Lewis acid (AlCl3) to form a salt.
    {tex}\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{NH}_{2}+\mathrm{AlCl}_{3}{/tex} {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} {tex}{C_6}{H_5}\mathop N\limits^+{H_2}AlCl_3^ - {/tex}
    As a result, N acquires a positive charge so, it acts as a strong deactivating group for electrophilic substitution reaction. Thus, aniline does not undergo Friedel-Crafts reaction.
  2. Primary aromatic amines cannot be prepared by Gabriel phthalimide synthesis because aryl halides do not undergo nucleophilic substitution with the anion formed by phthalimide. Gabriel phthalimide synthesis is used for the preparation of primary amines.
  3. Aliphatic amines are stronger bases than the ammonia because the alkyl group in aliphatic amines has +I effect. So the alkyl group tends to increase the electron density on the nitrogen atom whereas the electron releasing tendency of amines becomes more than that of ammonia.

options.explanation: NH 3​ < primary amine < tertiary amine < secondary amine This is because: Steric hindrance The size of an alkyl group is more than that of a hydrogen atom. So, an alkyl group would hinder the attack of a hydrogen atom, thus decreasing the basicity of the molecule. So, the more the number of alkyl groups attached, lesser will be its basicity. Solvation of ions When amines are dissolved in water, they form protonated amines. Also, the number of possibilities for hydrogen bonding also increases. More the number of hydrogen bonding more is the hydration that is released in the process of the formation of hydrogen bonds.

question_html:
1. How will you distinguish between the following pairs of compounds:
    1. Aniline and Ethanamine
    2. Aniline and N-methylaniline
  2. Arrange the following compounds in decreasing order of their boiling points: 
    Butanol, Butanamine, Butane
question_text: How will you distinguish between the following pairs of compounds: Aniline and Ethanamine Aniline and N-methylaniline Arrange the following compounds in decreasing order of their boiling points: Butanol, Butanamine, Butane
explanation: Ethylamine and aniline can be distinguished using the azo-dye test. A dye is obtained when aromatic amines react with {tex}HN{O_2}\left( {NaN{O_2} + dil.\,HCl} \right) {/tex} at {tex}0 - 5^\circ C{/tex} followed by a reaction with the alkaline solution of 2-naphthol. The dye is usually yellow, red, or orange in colour. Aliphatic amines give a brisk effervescence due to the evolution of {tex}{N_2}{/tex} gas under similar conditions. {tex}C{H_3}C{H_2} - N{H_2} + HONO\xrightarrow{{0 - 5^\circ }}{C_2}{H_5}OH + {N_2} \uparrow + {H_2}O{/tex} Aniline and N-ethylaniline can be distinguished using the Carbylamine test. Primary amines, on heating with chloroform and ethanolic potassium hydroxide form foul-smelling isocyanides or carbylamines. Aniline, being an aromatic primary amine, gives positive carbylamine test. However, N-methylaniline, being a secondary amine does not. The decreasing order of boiling point of following compound is Butanol > Butanmine > Butane.
solution: Ethylamine and aniline can be distinguished using the azo-dye test. A dye is obtained when aromatic amines react with {tex}HN{O_2}\left( {NaN{O_2} + dil.\,HCl} \right) {/tex} at {tex}0 - 5^\circ C{/tex} followed by a reaction with the alkaline solution of 2-naphthol. Primary amines, on heating with chloroform and ethanolic potassium hydroxide form foul-smelling isocyanides or carbylamines. Aniline, being an aromatic primary amine, gives positive carbylamine test. However, N-methylaniline, being a secondary amine does not. The decreasing order of boiling point of following compound is Butanol > Butanmine > Butane.

1. Ethylamine and aniline can be distinguished using the azo-dye test. A dye is obtained when aromatic amines react with {tex}HN{O_2}\left( {NaN{O_2} + dil.\,HCl} \right) {/tex} at {tex}0 - 5^\circ C{/tex} followed by a reaction with the alkaline solution of 2-naphthol. The dye is usually yellow, red, or orange in colour. Aliphatic amines give a brisk effervescence due to the evolution of {tex}{N_2}{/tex} gas under similar conditions.


      {tex}C{H_3}C{H_2} - N{H_2} + HONO\xrightarrow{{0 - 5^\circ }}{C_2}{H_5}OH + {N_2} \uparrow + {H_2}O{/tex}
    2. Aniline and N-ethylaniline can be distinguished using the Carbylamine test. Primary amines, on heating with chloroform and ethanolic potassium hydroxide form foul-smelling isocyanides or carbylamines. Aniline, being an aromatic primary amine, gives positive carbylamine test. However, N-methylaniline, being a secondary amine does not.

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
1. Give reasons:
    1. Although – NH2 group is o/p directing in electrophilic substitution reactions, yet aniline, on nitration gives good yield of m-nitroaniline.
    2. (CH3)2 NH is more basic than (CH3)3 N in an aqueous solution.
    3. Ammonolysis of alkyl halides is not a good method to prepare pure primary amines.
  2. Distinguish between the following:
    1. CH3CH2NH2 and (CH3CH2)2 NH
    2. Aniline and CH3NH2
question_text: Give reasons: Although – NH 2 group is o/p directing in electrophilic substitution reactions, yet aniline, on nitration gives good yield of m-nitroaniline. (CH 3 ) 2 NH is more basic than (CH 3 ) 3 N in an aqueous solution. Ammonolysis of alkyl halides is not a good method to prepare pure primary amines. Distinguish between the following: CH 3 CH 2 NH 2 and (CH 3 CH 2 ) 2 NH Aniline and CH 3 NH 2
explanation: Nitration is carried out in acidic medium. In an acidic medium, aniline is protonated to form the anilinium ion which is meta directing. That is why besides the ortho and para derivatives, a substantial amount of meta derivative (m-nitroaniline) is also formed. (CH 3 ) 2 NH is a secondary amine and (CH 3 ) 3 N is a tertiary amine. Tertiary amine due to the presence of three alkyl groups is more hindered than secondary amine which has only two alkyl groups attached to it. Therefore formation of ammonium ion is easier in secondary amine than the tertiary amine. Therefore, it makes secondary amine less basic than the tertiary amine. The ammonolysis of alkyl halide leads to the formation of the mixture of primary, secondary and tertiary amine along with the formation of quaternary salt. It is very difficult to separate pure primary amine from this mixture. Test CH 3 CH 2 NH 2 (CH 3 CH 2 ) 2 NH Carbylamine test (add chloroform and alcoholic KOH to both the compounds separately in a test tube) Forms a foul-smelling compound (gives positive test) No reaction take place (gives negative test) Azo dye Test Aniline Methyl Amine (CH 3 NH 2 ) Add a small amount of nitrous acid with aq. HCl Forms a yellow coloured dye (gives positive test) No dye is formed(gives negative test)
solution: Nitration is carried out in acidic medium. In an acidic medium, aniline is protonated to form the anilinium ion which is meta directing. That is why besides the ortho and para derivatives, a substantial amount of meta derivative (m-nitroaniline) is also formed. (CH 3 ) 2 NH is a secondary amine and (CH 3 ) 3 N is a tertiary amine. Tertiary amine due to the presence of three alkyl groups is more hindered than secondary amine which has only two alkyl groups attached to it. Therefore formation of ammonium ion is easier in secondary amine than the tertiary amine. Therefore, it makes secondary amine less basic than the tertiary amine. The ammonolysis of alkyl halide leads to the formation of the mixture of primary, secondary and tertiary amine along with the formation of quaternary salt. It is very difficult to separate pure primary amine from this mixture. Test CH 3 CH 2 NH 2 (CH 3 CH 2 ) 2 NH Carbylamine test (add chloroform and alcoholic KOH to both the compounds separately in a test tube) Forms a foul-smelling compound (gives positive test) No reaction take place (gives negative test) Azo dye Test Aniline Methyl Amine (CH 3 NH 2 ) Add a small amount of nitrous acid with aq. HCl Forms a yellow coloured dye (gives positive test) No dye is formed(gives negative test)
solution_html:
1. Nitration is carried out in acidic medium. In an acidic medium, aniline is protonated to form the anilinium ion which is meta directing. That is why besides the ortho and para derivatives, a substantial amount of meta derivative (m-nitroaniline) is also formed. 
    2. (CH3)2NH is a secondary amine and (CH3)3N is a tertiary amine. Tertiary amine due to the presence of three alkyl groups is more hindered than secondary amine which has only two alkyl groups attached to it. Therefore formation of ammonium ion is easier in secondary amine than the tertiary amine. Therefore, it makes secondary amine less basic than the tertiary amine.
    3. The ammonolysis of alkyl halide leads to the formation of the mixture of primary, secondary and tertiary amine along with the formation of quaternary salt. It is very difficult to separate pure primary amine from this mixture.
    1. Test CH3CH2NH2 (CH3CH2)2NH
      Carbylamine test (add chloroform and alcoholic KOH to both the compounds separately in a test tube) Forms a foul-smelling compound (gives positive test) No reaction take place (gives negative test)
    2. Azo dye Test Aniline Methyl Amine (CH3NH2)
      Add a small amount of nitrous acid with aq. HCl Forms a yellow coloured dye (gives positive test) No dye is formed(gives negative test)

question_html:
Give the structures of A and B in the following sequence of reactions:

  1. {tex}C{H_3}COOH\xrightarrow[\Delta ]{{N{H_3}}}A\xrightarrow{{NaOBr}}B{/tex}
  2. {tex}{C_6}{H_5}N{O_2}\xrightarrow{{Fe/HCl}}A\xrightarrow[{{0^o} - {5^o}C}]{{NaN{O_2} + HCl}}B{/tex}
  3. {tex}{C_6}{H_5}N_2^ + C{l^ - }\xrightarrow[\Delta ]{{CuCN}}A\xrightarrow{{{H_2}O/{H^ + }}}B{/tex}
question_text: Give the structures of A and B in the following sequence of reactions: {tex}C{H_3}COOH\xrightarrow[\Delta ]{{N{H_3}}}A\xrightarrow{{NaOBr}}B{/tex} {tex}{C_6}{H_5}N{O_2}\xrightarrow{{Fe/HCl}}A\xrightarrow[{{0^o} - {5^o}C}]{{NaN{O_2} + HCl}}B{/tex} {tex}{C_6}{H_5}N_2^ + C{l^ - }\xrightarrow[\Delta ]{{CuCN}}A\xrightarrow{{{H_2}O/{H^ + }}}B{/tex}
explanation: {tex}C{H_3}COOH\xrightarrow[\Delta ]{{N{H_3}}}C{H_3}CONH_2\xrightarrow{{NaOBr}}C{H_3}NH_2{/tex} {tex}{C_6}{H_5}N{O_2}\xrightarrow{{Fe/HCl}}{C_6}{H_5}NH_2\xrightarrow[{{0^o} - {5^o}C}]{{NaN{O_2} + HCl}}{C_6}{H_5}{N_2}Cl{/tex} {tex}{C_6}{H_5}N_2^ + C{l^ - }\xrightarrow[\Delta ]{{CuCN}}{C_6}{H_5}CN\xrightarrow{{{H_2}O/{H^ + }}}{C_6}{H_5}OH{/tex}
solution: {tex}C{H_3}COOH\xrightarrow[\Delta ]{{N{H_3}}}C{H_3}CONH_2\xrightarrow{{NaOBr}}C{H_3}NH_2{/tex} {tex}{C_6}{H_5}N{O_2}\xrightarrow{{Fe/HCl}}{C_6}{H_5}NH_2\xrightarrow[{{0^o} - {5^o}C}]{{NaN{O_2} + HCl}}{C_6}{H_5}{N_2}Cl{/tex} {tex}{C_6}{H_5}N_2^ + C{l^ - }\xrightarrow[\Delta ]{{CuCN}}{C_6}{H_5}CN\xrightarrow{{{H_2}O/{H^ + }}}{C_6}{H_5}OH{/tex}
solution_html:
1. {tex}C{H_3}COOH\xrightarrow[\Delta ]{{N{H_3}}}C{H_3}CONH_2\xrightarrow{{NaOBr}}C{H_3}NH_2{/tex}
  2. {tex}{C_6}{H_5}N{O_2}\xrightarrow{{Fe/HCl}}{C_6}{H_5}NH_2\xrightarrow[{{0^o} - {5^o}C}]{{NaN{O_2} + HCl}}{C_6}{H_5}{N_2}Cl{/tex}
  3. {tex}{C_6}{H_5}N_2^ + C{l^ - }\xrightarrow[\Delta ]{{CuCN}}{C_6}{H_5}CN\xrightarrow{{{H_2}O/{H^ + }}}{C_6}{H_5}OH{/tex}

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
Arrange the following compounds as directed:

  1. In increasing order of solubility in water:
    (CH3)2NH, CH3NH2, C6H5NH2
  2. In decreasing order of basic strength in aqueous solution:
    (CH3)3N, (CH3)2NH, CH3NH2
  3. In increasing order of boiling point:
    (C2H5)2NH, (C2H5)3N, C2H5NH2
question_text: Arrange the following compounds as directed: In increasing order of solubility in water: (CH 3 ) 2 NH, CH 3 NH 2 , C 6 H 5 NH 2 In decreasing order of basic strength in aqueous solution: (CH 3 ) 3 N, (CH 3 ) 2 NH, CH 3 NH 2 In increasing order of boiling point: (C 2 H 5 ) 2 NH, (C 2 H 5 ) 3 N, C 2 H 5 NH 2
explanation: C 6 H 5 NH 2 < (CH 2 ) 2 NH < CH 3 NH 2 (CH 3 ) 2 NH > CH 3 NH 2 > (CH 3 ) 3 N (C 2 H 5 ) 3 N < (C 2 H 5 ) 2 NH < C 2 H 5 NH 2
solution: C 6 H 5 NH 2 < (CH 2 ) 2 NH < CH 3 NH 2 (CH 3 ) 2 NH > CH 3 NH 2 > (CH 3 ) 3 N (C 2 H 5 ) 3 N < (C 2 H 5 ) 2 NH < C 2 H 5 NH 2
solution_html:
1. C6H5NH2 < (CH2)2NH < CH3NH2
  2. (CH3)2NH > CH3NH2 > (CH3)3N
  3. (C2H5)3N < (C2H5)2NH < C2H5NH2

subject: Chemistry
question_html: Out of (CH3)3N and (CH3)2NH, which one is more basic in aqueous solution?
question_text: Out of (CH 3 ) 3 N and (CH 3 ) 2 NH, which one is more basic in aqueous solution?
explanation: (CH 3 ) 2 NH = Secondary amine (CH 3 ) 3 -N = Tertiary amine Secondary amines are more basic than tertiary amines in aqueous solution due to steric factor. So, (CH 3 ) 2 NH is more basic in aqueous solution.
solution: (CH 3 ) 2 NH = Secondary amine (CH 3 ) 3 -N = Tertiary amine Secondary amines are more basic than tertiary amines in aqueous solution due to steric factor. So, (CH 3 ) 2 NH is more basic in aqueous solution.
solution_html:
(CH3)2NH = Secondary amine
(CH3)3-N = Tertiary amine
Secondary amines are more basic than tertiary amines in aqueous solution due to steric factor. So, (CH3)2NH is more basic in aqueous solution.

explanation: Isomer of C 3 H 9 N which does not react with Hinsberg reagent is (CH 3 ) 3 N, i.e., tertiary amine. {tex}\underset{\mathrm{Trimethylamine}}{{\mathrm{CH}}_3-\underset{{\mathrm{CH}}_3}{\overset{\cdot\cdot}{\underset\vert{\mathrm N}}}-{\mathrm{CH}}_3}+\underset{\mathrm{Hinsberg}\\\mathrm{reagent}}{{\mathrm C}_6{\mathrm H}_5{\mathrm{SO}}_2\mathrm{Cl}}\;\rightarrow\mathrm{No}\;\mathrm{reaction}{/tex}
solution: Isomer of C 3 H 9 N which does not react with Hinsberg reagent is (CH 3 ) 3 N, i.e., tertiary amine. {tex}\underset{\mathrm{Trimethylamine}}{{\mathrm{CH}}_3-\underset{{\mathrm{CH}}_3}{\overset{\cdot\cdot}{\underset\vert{\mathrm N}}}-{\mathrm{CH}}_3}+\underset{\mathrm{Hinsberg}\\\mathrm{reagent}}{{\mathrm C}_6{\mathrm H}_5{\mathrm{SO}}_2\mathrm{Cl}}\;\rightarrow\mathrm{No}\;\mathrm{reaction}{/tex}
solution_html:
Isomer of C3H9N which does not react with Hinsberg reagent is (CH3)3N, i.e., tertiary amine.
{tex}\underset{\mathrm{Trimethylamine}}{{\mathrm{CH}}_3-\underset{{\mathrm{CH}}_3}{\overset{\cdot\cdot}{\underset\vert{\mathrm N}}}-{\mathrm{CH}}_3}+\underset{\mathrm{Hinsberg}\\\mathrm{reagent}}{{\mathrm C}_6{\mathrm H}_5{\mathrm{SO}}_2\mathrm{Cl}}\;\rightarrow\mathrm{No}\;\mathrm{reaction}{/tex}

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
1. Give one chemical test to distinguish between the compounds of the following pairs:
    1. CH3NH2 and (CH3)2NH
    2. Aniline and Ethanamine
  2. Why aniline does not undergo Friedel-Crafts reaction?
question_text: Give one chemical test to distinguish between the compounds of the following pairs: CH 3 NH 2 and (CH 3 ) 2 NH Aniline and Ethanamine Why aniline does not undergo Friedel-Crafts reaction?
explanation: CH 3 NH 2 with Hinsberg’s Reagent (Benzene Sulphonyl Chloride) forms a precipitate which is soluble in base. (CH 3 ) 2 NH with Hinsberg’s Reagent (Benzene Sulphonyl Chloride) forms a precipitate which is insoluble in base. Add benzene diazoniumchloride to both the compounds, aniline forms yellow dye while ethanamine does not. Due to salt formation between Aniline and anhydrous AlCl 3 . Aniline behave as lewis base and anhydrous AlCl 3 behaves as lewis acid. Nitrogen of aniline acquires positive charge which acts as a strong deactivating group. These are the reasons thats why aniline does not undergo Friedel-Crafts reaction.
solution: CH 3 NH 2 with Hinsberg’s Reagent (Benzene Sulphonyl Chloride) forms a precipitate which is soluble in base. (CH 3 ) 2 NH with Hinsberg’s Reagent (Benzene Sulphonyl Chloride) forms a precipitate which is insoluble in base. Add benzene diazoniumchloride to both the compounds, aniline forms yellow dye while ethanamine does not. Due to salt formation between Aniline and anhydrous AlCl 3 . Aniline behave as lewis base and anhydrous AlCl 3 behaves as lewis acid. Nitrogen of aniline acquires positive charge which acts as a strong deactivating group. These are the reasons thats why aniline does not undergo Friedel-Crafts reaction.
solution_html:
1. CH3NH2 with Hinsberg’s Reagent (Benzene Sulphonyl Chloride) forms a precipitate which is soluble in base.
      (CH3)2NH with Hinsberg’s Reagent (Benzene Sulphonyl Chloride) forms a precipitate which is insoluble in base.
    2. Add benzene diazoniumchloride to both the compounds, aniline forms yellow dye while ethanamine does not.
  2. Due to salt formation between Aniline and anhydrous AlCl3.
    Aniline behave as lewis base and anhydrous AlCl3 behaves as lewis acid.
    Nitrogen of aniline acquires positive charge which acts as a strong deactivating group.
    These are the reasons thats why aniline does not undergo Friedel-Crafts reaction.

It can be shown in the form of chemical reaction as: CH 3 NH 2 {tex}\xrightarrow{\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}}{/tex} CH 3 NH 3 + + OH - FeCl 3 {tex}\xrightarrow{3 \mathrm{OH}^{-}}{/tex} Fe(OH) 3 {tex}\downarrow{/tex} + 3Cl -

It can be shown in the form of chemical reaction as: CH 3 NH 2 {tex}\xrightarrow{\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}}{/tex} CH 3 NH 3 + + OH - FeCl 3 {tex}\xrightarrow{3 \mathrm{OH}^{-}}{/tex} Fe(OH) 3 {tex}\downarrow{/tex} + 3Cl -

1. Due to resonance stabilisation of arene diazonium ion /
  2. Methyl amine being basic, gains a proton from water and releases hydroxyl ions which precipitate hydrated ferric oxide. It can be shown in the form of chemical reaction as:

CH 3 -COOH {tex}\xrightarrow{NH_3/\Delta}{/tex} A {tex}\xrightarrow{Br_2/KOH(aq)}{/tex} B {tex}\xrightarrow{CHCl_3 + alc.KOH}{/tex} C {tex}{C_6}{H_5}N_2^ + BF_4^ - {/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow[\Delta]{NaNO_2/Cu}{/tex} A {tex}\xrightarrow{Fe/HCl}{/tex} B {tex}\xrightarrow{CH_3COCl/pyridine}{/tex} C
explanation: {tex}\begin{array}{l}{\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}} \\ {\text { Acetic acid }}\end{array}{/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow[-H_2O]{NH_3/\Delta}{/tex} {tex}\mathop {\mathop {C{H_3}CON{H_2}}\limits_{\begin{array}{*{20}{l}} {Acetamide} \end{array}} }\limits_{(A)} {/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow[Hofmann\ bromamide\ degradation]{Br_2/KOH(aq)}{/tex} {tex}\mathop {\mathop {C{H_3}N{H_2}}\limits_{\begin{array}{*{20}{l}} {\begin{array}{*{20}{l}} {Methylamine} \end{array}} \end{array}} }\limits_{(B)} {/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow[Carbylamine\ reaction]{CHCI_3\ +\ alc\ KOH}{/tex} {tex}\mathop {\mathop {C{H_3}N{C}}\limits_{\begin{array}{*{20}{l}} {\begin{array}{*{20}{l}} {​​Methyl\ isocyanide} \end{array}} \end{array}} }\limits_{(C)} {/tex} {tex}{C_6}{H_5}N_2^ + BF_4^ - {/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow[N_2,\ -NaBF_4]{NaNO_2/Cu,\Delta}{/tex} {tex}\mathop {\mathop {C_6{H_5}N{O_2}}\limits_{\begin{array}{*{20}{l}} {Nitrobenzene} \end{array}} }\limits_{(A)} {/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow[Reduction]{Fe/HCl}{/tex} {tex}\mathop {\mathop {{C_6}{H_5}N{H_2}}\limits_{\begin{array}{*{20}{l}} {\begin{array}{*{20}{l}} {Aniline} \end{array}} \end{array}} }\limits_{(B)} {/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow[-HCl]{CH_3COCl/pyridine}{/tex} {tex}\mathop {\mathop {{C_6}{H_5}NHCOC{H_3}}\limits_{\begin{array}{*{20}{l}} {\begin{array}{*{20}{l}} {Acetanilide} \end{array}} \end{array}} }\limits_{(C)} {/tex}

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
1. Give reasons for the following :
    1. Acetylation of aniline reduces its activation effect. 
    2. CH3NH2 is more basic than C6H5NH2. 
    3. Although -NH2 is o/p-directing group, yet aniline on nitration gives a significant amount of m-nitroaniline.
  2. Explain the following reactions:
    1. Coupling reaction
    2. Gabriel phthalimide reaction
question_text: Give reasons for the following : Acetylation of aniline reduces its activation effect. CH 3 NH 2 is more basic than C 6 H 5 NH 2 . Although -NH 2 is o/p-directing group, yet aniline on nitration gives a significant amount of m-nitroaniline. Explain the following reactions: Coupling reaction Gabriel phthalimide reaction
explanation: Due to the electron-withdrawing effect of the acetyl group, the lone pair of electrons on N-atom is attracted by the acetyl group. As a result, the lone pair of electrons on N-atom is not exclusively available for donation to the benzene ring and hence, activating the effect of the - NH 2 group is reduced. In aromatic amines, the lone pair of electrons present on nitrogen takes part in resonance and hence, not available for donation. Alsoaniline is more stable than anilinium ion. Hence aniline has a very little tendency to accept a proton to form anilinium ion. However, in aliphatic amines, the lone pair is easily available for donation. That's why aliphatic amines are more basic than aromatic amines. Nitration is usually carried out with a mixture of conc. HNO 3 and cone. H 2 SO 4 . So in the presence of these acids, aniline gets protonated to form anilinium ion. Therefore, the reaction mixture consists of aniline + and anilinium ion. -NH 2 group in aniline is o,p-directing and activating, whereas the {tex}_{\mathrm{NH}_{3}}^{+}{/tex} group in anilinium ism-directing and deactivating. Now, nitration of aniline mainly gives p-nitroaniline due to steric hindrance at a-position and the nitration of anilinium ion gives m-nitroaniline. Gabriel phthalimide reaction Phthalimide on treatment with ethanolic KOHgives potassium phthalimide which on heating with a suitable alkyl halide gives N-substituted phthalimide, which upon subsequent hydrolysis with alkali gives primary amines. This reaction is called Gabriel phthalimide reaction. Coupling reaction Arenediazonium salts react with highly reactive (i.e. electron-rich) aromatic compounds such as aniline, phenols to form brightly coloured azo compounds, Ar-N = N-Ar. This reaction is called a coupling reaction. e.g. Benzene diazonium chloride reacts with aniline in faintly acidic medium (pH 4- 5) at 273·278K, in which the molecule at its para-position is coupled with the diazonium salt to form p-aminoazobenzene. This is an example of a coupling reaction.
solution: Due to the electron-withdrawing effect of the acetyl group, the lone pair of electrons on N-atom is attracted by the acetyl group. As a result, the lone pair of electrons on N-atom is not exclusively available for donation to the benzene ring and hence, activating the effect of the - NH 2 group is reduced. In aromatic amines, the lone pair of electrons present on nitrogen takes part in resonance and hence, not available for donation. Alsoaniline is more stable than anilinium ion. Hence aniline has a very little tendency to accept a proton to form anilinium ion. However, in aliphatic amines, the lone pair is easily available for donation. That's why aliphatic amines are more basic than aromatic amines. Nitration is usually carried out with a mixture of conc. HNO 3 and cone. H 2 SO 4 . So in the presence of these acids, aniline gets protonated to form anilinium ion. Therefore, the reaction mixture consists of aniline + and anilinium ion. -NH 2 group in aniline is o,p-directing and activating, whereas the {tex}_{\mathrm{NH}_{3}}^{+}{/tex} group in anilinium ism-directing and deactivating. Now, nitration of aniline mainly gives p-nitroaniline due to steric hindrance at a-position and the nitration of anilinium ion gives m-nitroaniline. Gabriel phthalimide reaction Phthalimide on treatment with ethanolic KOHgives potassium phthalimide which on heating with a suitable alkyl halide gives N-substituted phthalimide, which upon subsequent hydrolysis with alkali gives primary amines. This reaction is called Gabriel phthalimide reaction. Coupling reaction Arenediazonium salts react with highly reactive (i.e. electron-rich) aromatic compounds such as aniline, phenols to form brightly coloured azo compounds, Ar-N = N-Ar. This reaction is called a coupling reaction. e.g. Benzene diazonium chloride reacts with aniline in faintly acidic medium (pH 4- 5) at 273·278K, in which the molecule at its para-position is coupled with the diazonium salt to form p-aminoazobenzene. This is an example of a coupling reaction.
solution_html:
1. Due to the electron-withdrawing effect of the acetyl group, the lone pair of electrons on N-atom is attracted by the acetyl group. As a result, the lone pair of electrons on N-atom is not exclusively available for donation to the benzene ring and hence, activating the effect of the - NH2 group is reduced.
    2. In aromatic amines, the lone pair of electrons present on nitrogen takes part in resonance and hence, not available for donation. Alsoaniline is more stable than anilinium ion. Hence aniline has a very little tendency to accept a proton to form anilinium ion. However, in aliphatic amines, the lone pair is easily available for donation. That's why aliphatic amines are more basic than aromatic amines.
    3. Nitration is usually carried out with a mixture of conc. HNO3 and cone. H2SO4. So in the presence of these acids, aniline gets protonated to form anilinium ion. Therefore, the reaction mixture consists of aniline + and anilinium ion. -NH2 group in aniline is o,p-directing and activating, whereas the {tex}_{\mathrm{NH}_{3}}^{+}{/tex}group in anilinium ism-directing and deactivating. Now, nitration of aniline mainly gives p-nitroaniline due to steric hindrance at a-position and the nitration of anilinium ion gives m-nitroaniline. 

    1. Gabriel phthalimide reaction Phthalimide on treatment with ethanolic KOHgives potassium phthalimide which on heating with a suitable alkyl halide gives N-substituted phthalimide, which upon subsequent hydrolysis with alkali gives primary amines.

      This reaction is called Gabriel phthalimide reaction.
    2. Coupling reaction Arenediazonium salts react with highly reactive (i.e. electron-rich) aromatic compounds such as aniline, phenols to form brightly coloured azo compounds, Ar-N = N-Ar. This reaction is called a coupling reaction. e.g. Benzene diazonium chloride reacts with aniline in faintly acidic medium (pH 4- 5) at 273·278K, in which the molecule at its para-position is coupled with the diazonium salt to form p-aminoazobenzene.
      This is an example of a coupling reaction.

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
Give the reasons for the following:

  1. Aniline does not undergo Friedel-Crafts reaction.
  2. (CH3)2NH is more basic than (CH3)3.N in an aqueous solution.
  3. Primary amines have a higher boiling point than tertiary amines.
question_text: Give the reasons for the following: Aniline does not undergo Friedel-Crafts reaction. (CH 3 ) 2 NH is more basic than (CH 3 ) 3 .N in an aqueous solution. Primary amines have a higher boiling point than tertiary amines.
explanation: Aniline is a Lewis base and forms a salt with Lewis acid. Aniline being a Lewis base reacts with Lewis acid (AlCl 3 )to form a salt. {tex}\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{NH}_{2}+\mathrm{AlCl}_{3}{/tex} {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} {tex}{C_6}{H_5}\mathop N\limits^+{H_2}AlCl_3^ - {/tex} As a result, N acquires a positive charge so, it acts as a strong deactivating group for electrophilic substitution reaction. Thus, aniline does not undergo Friedel-Crafts reaction. In aqueous solution, basic nature depends on + I-effect, H-bonding, and steric-effect. The combined effect shows that (CH 3 ) 2 ·NH is more basic than (CH 3 ) 3 ·N as H-bonding is more in case of (CH 3 ) 2 ·NH than in (CH 3 ) 3 N, which predominates over the stability due to +I- effect of three -CH 3 groups. Large pK b value means a weak base In aniline, the lone pair of electrons on N-atom is delocalized over the benzene ring. As a result, electron density on the nitrogen decreases and electrons are not available for donation. In contrast, in CH 3 -NH 2 , +I effect of -CH 3 group increases the electron density on the N-atom. Therefore, aniline is a weaker base than methylamine and hence, its pKb value is higher than that of methylamine.
solution: Aniline is a Lewis base and forms a salt with Lewis acid. Aniline being a Lewis base reacts with Lewis acid (AlCl 3 )to form a salt. {tex}\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{NH}_{2}+\mathrm{AlCl}_{3}{/tex} {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} {tex}{C_6}{H_5}\mathop N\limits^+{H_2}AlCl_3^ - {/tex} As a result, N acquires a positive charge so, it acts as a strong deactivating group for electrophilic substitution reaction. Thus, aniline does not undergo Friedel-Crafts reaction. In aqueous solution, basic nature depends on + I-effect, H-bonding, and steric-effect. The combined effect shows that (CH 3 ) 2 ·NH is more basic than (CH 3 ) 3 ·N as H-bonding is more in case of (CH 3 ) 2 ·NH than in (CH 3 ) 3 N, which predominates over the stability due to +I- effect of three -CH 3 groups. Large pK b value means a weak base In aniline, the lone pair of electrons on N-atom is delocalized over the benzene ring. As a result, electron density on the nitrogen decreases and electrons are not available for donation. In contrast, in CH 3 -NH 2 , +I effect of -CH 3 group increases the electron density on the N-atom. Therefore, aniline is a weaker base than methylamine and hence, its pKb value is higher than that of methylamine.
solution_html:
1. Aniline is a Lewis base and forms a salt with Lewis acid.
    Aniline being a Lewis base reacts with Lewis acid (AlCl3)to form a salt.
    {tex}\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{NH}_{2}+\mathrm{AlCl}_{3}{/tex} {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} {tex}{C_6}{H_5}\mathop N\limits^+{H_2}AlCl_3^ - {/tex}
    As a result, N acquires a positive charge so, it acts as a strong deactivating group for electrophilic substitution reaction. Thus, aniline does not undergo Friedel-Crafts reaction.
  2. In aqueous solution, basic nature depends on + I-effect, H-bonding, and steric-effect.
    The combined effect shows that (CH3)2·NH is more basic than (CH3)3·N as H-bonding is more in case of (CH3)2·NH than in (CH3)3N, which predominates over the stability due to +I- effect of three -CH3 groups.
  3. Large pKb value means a weak base
    In aniline, the lone pair of electrons on N-atom is delocalized over the benzene ring. As a result, electron density on the nitrogen decreases and electrons are not available for donation. In contrast, in CH3-NH2, +I effect of -CH3 group increases the electron density on the N-atom. Therefore, aniline is a weaker base than methylamine and hence, its pKb value is higher than that of methylamine.

subject: Chemistry
question_html: Describe a method for the identification of primary, secondary and tertiary amines. Also write chemical equations of the reactions involved.
question_text: Describe a method for the identification of primary, secondary and tertiary amines. Also write chemical equations of the reactions involved.
explanation: Primary, secondary and tertiary amines can be identified and distinguished by Hinsberg's test. In this test, the amines are allowed to react with Hinsberg's reagent (benzene sulphonyl chloride C 6 H 5 SO 2 Cl). Primary amines react with benzenesulphonyl chloride to form N-alkyl benzenesulphonamide which is soluble in alkali. Secondary amines react with Hinsberg's reagent to form N, N-dialkyl benzene sulphonamide which is insoluble in alkali. Tertiary amines do not react with Hinsberg's reagent.
solution: Primary, secondary and tertiary amines can be identified and distinguished by Hinsberg's test. In this test, the amines are allowed to react with Hinsberg's reagent (benzene sulphonyl chloride C 6 H 5 SO 2 Cl). Primary amines react with benzenesulphonyl chloride to form N-alkyl benzenesulphonamide which is soluble in alkali. Secondary amines react with Hinsberg's reagent to form N, N-dialkyl benzene sulphonamide which is insoluble in alkali. Tertiary amines do not react with Hinsberg's reagent.
solution_html:
Primary, secondary and tertiary amines can be identified and distinguished by Hinsberg's test. In this test, the amines are allowed to react with Hinsberg's reagent (benzene sulphonyl chloride C6H5SO2Cl).

  1. Primary amines react with benzenesulphonyl chloride to form N-alkyl benzenesulphonamide which is soluble in alkali.
  2. Secondary amines react with Hinsberg's reagent to form N, N-dialkyl benzene sulphonamide which is insoluble in alkali.
  3. Tertiary amines do not react with Hinsberg's reagent.

C6H5−NH2 and C6H5−NH−CH3
question_text: Give a simple chemical test to distinguish between the following pair of compounds. C 6 H 5 −NH 2 and C 6 H 5 −NH−CH 3
explanation: The given amines are primary and secondary aromatic amines respectively. In order to distinguish between primary and secondary amine, the amine is shaken with benzene sulphonyl chloride (Hinsberg's reagent) in the presence of excess of aqueous KOH solution. A primary amine gives a soluble N-benzyl sulphonamide as hydrogen attached to nitrogen in sulphonamide is strongly acidic. A secondary amine gives an insoluble N, N-dialkylbenzene sulphonamide. It does not contain any hydrogen atom attached to nitrogen atom. It is not acidic and hence insoluble in alkali.

explanation: The relative basic strength of C 2 H 5 NH 2 , (C 2 H5) 2 NH and (C 2 H 5 ) 3 N depends upon the stabilisation of their corresponding conjugate acids (formed as a result of accepting a proton from water by a number of factors such a; B-bonding, steric hindrance of the alkyl groups and +I-effect of the alkyl groups. All these factors are favourable for 2 o amines, therefore, ((C 2 H5) 2 NH is a stronger base than C 2 H 5 NH 2 and ((C 2 H 5 ) 3 N. Since. C 2 H 5 group is large, it exerts some steric hindrance to H-bond. Hence, the overall basic strength of the three amines increases in the order: C 2 H 5 NH 2 < (C 2 H 5 ) 3 N < (C 2 H5) 2 NH.
