subject: Chemistry
question_html:
1. Illustrate the following reactions giving suitable example in each case:
    1. Ammonolysis
    2. Coupling reaction
    3. Acetylation of amines
  2. Describe Hinsberg method for the identification of primary, secondary and tertiary amines. Also, write the chemical equations of the reactions involved.
question_text: Illustrate the following reactions giving suitable example in each case: Ammonolysis Coupling reaction Acetylation of amines Describe Hinsberg method for the identification of primary, secondary and tertiary amines. Also, write the chemical equations of the reactions involved.
explanation: Ammonolysis The process of cleavage of the C-X bond by ammonia molecule is known as ammonolysis. In this process, an alkyl or benzyl halide on reaction with an ethanolic solution of ammonia undergoes nucleophilic substitution reaction in which halogen atom is replaced by an amino (-NH 2 ) group. The reaction is carried out in a sealed tube at 373 K. The primary amine thus obtained behaves as a nucleophile and can further react with alkyl halide to form secondary and tertiary amines and further quaternary salts. R−NH 2 {tex}\xrightarrow{RX}{/tex} R 2 NH {tex}\xrightarrow{RX}{/tex} XR 3 N {tex}\xrightarrow{RX}{/tex} R 4 {tex}\mathop N\limits^ + {/tex} X - Coupling reaction Arenediazonium salts react with highly reactive (i.e. electron-rich) aromatic compounds such as aniline, phenols to form brightly coloured azo compounds, Ar-N =N-Ar. This reaction is called coupling reaction. e.g. Benzene diazonium chloride reacts with aniline in faintly acidic medium (pH 4- 5) at 273·278K, in which the molecule at its para-position is coupled with the diazonium salt to form p-aminoazobenzene. This is an example of coupling reaction. Acetylation of amines The process of introducing an acetyl group {tex}\left( {C{H_3} - \mathop {\mathop C\limits^{||} }\limits^O - } \right){/tex} into the molecule is called acetylation of amines. The reaction of primary and secondary amines with benzenesulphonyl chloride (C 6 H 5 SO 2 Cl, known as Hinsberg's reagent to form sulphonamides is known as Hinsberg's method (or reaction). This method (or reaction) is used for separating 1 o , 2 o and 3 o amines. The reaction of benzenesulphonyl chloride with primary amine yields N-ethylbenzenesulphonamide. The hydrogen attached to N-atom in sulphonamide is strogly acidic due to the presence of strong electron withdrawing sulphonyl group. Hence, it is soluble in alkali. In the reaction with secondary amine, N, N-diethylbenzenes amide is formed. Since, N, N-diethylbenzenesulphonamide does not contain any hydrogen atom attached to nitrogen atom, it is not acidic and hence, insoluble in alkali. Tertiary amines do not react with benzenesulphonyl chloride. as it doesn't contain replaceable hydrogens.
solution: Ammonolysis The process of cleavage of the C-X bond by ammonia molecule is known as ammonolysis. In this process, an alkyl or benzyl halide on reaction with an ethanolic solution of ammonia undergoes nucleophilic substitution reaction in which halogen atom is replaced by an amino (-NH 2 ) group. The reaction is carried out in a sealed tube at 373 K. The primary amine thus obtained behaves as a nucleophile and can further react with alkyl halide to form secondary and tertiary amines and further quaternary salts. R−NH 2 {tex}\xrightarrow{RX}{/tex} R 2 NH {tex}\xrightarrow{RX}{/tex} XR 3 N {tex}\xrightarrow{RX}{/tex} R 4 {tex}\mathop N\limits^ + {/tex} X - Coupling reaction Arenediazonium salts react with highly reactive (i.e. electron-rich) aromatic compounds such as aniline, phenols to form brightly coloured azo compounds, Ar-N =N-Ar. This reaction is called coupling reaction. e.g. Benzene diazonium chloride reacts with aniline in faintly acidic medium (pH 4- 5) at 273·278K, in which the molecule at its para-position is coupled with the diazonium salt to form p-aminoazobenzene. This is an example of coupling reaction. Acetylation of amines The process of introducing an acetyl group {tex}\left( {C{H_3} - \mathop {\mathop C\limits^{||} }\limits^O - } \right){/tex} into the molecule is called acetylation of amines. The reaction of primary and secondary amines with benzenesulphonyl chloride (C 6 H 5 SO 2 Cl, known as Hinsberg's reagent to form sulphonamides is known as Hinsberg's method (or reaction). This method (or reaction) is used for separating 1 o , 2 o and 3 o amines. The reaction of benzenesulphonyl chloride with primary amine yields N-ethylbenzenesulphonamide. The hydrogen attached to N-atom in sulphonamide is strogly acidic due to the presence of strong electron withdrawing sulphonyl group. Hence, it is soluble in alkali. In the reaction with secondary amine, N, N-diethylbenzenes amide is formed. Since, N, N-diethylbenzenesulphonamide does not contain any hydrogen atom attached to nitrogen atom, it is not acidic and hence, insoluble in alkali. Tertiary amines do not react with benzenesulphonyl chloride. as it doesn't contain replaceable hydrogens.
solution_html:
1.  
    1. Ammonolysis The process of cleavage of the C-X bond by ammonia molecule is known as ammonolysis. In this process, an alkyl or benzyl halide on reaction with an ethanolic solution of ammonia undergoes nucleophilic substitution reaction in which halogen atom is replaced by an amino (-NH2) group. The reaction is carried out in a sealed tube at 373 K. The primary amine thus obtained behaves as a nucleophile and can further react with alkyl halide to form secondary and tertiary amines and further quaternary salts. 
       R−NH2 {tex}\xrightarrow{RX}{/tex}R2NH{tex}\xrightarrow{RX}{/tex} XR3N {tex}\xrightarrow{RX}{/tex} R4{tex}\mathop N\limits^ + {/tex}X-
    2. Coupling reaction Arenediazonium salts react with highly reactive (i.e. electron-rich) aromatic compounds such as aniline, phenols to form brightly coloured azo compounds, Ar-N =N-Ar. This reaction is called coupling reaction. e.g. Benzene diazonium chloride reacts with aniline in faintly acidic medium (pH 4- 5) at 273·278K, in which the molecule at its para-position is coupled with the diazonium salt to form p-aminoazobenzene. This is an example of coupling reaction.
    3. Acetylation of amines The process of introducing an acetyl group {tex}\left( {C{H_3} - \mathop {\mathop C\limits^{||} }\limits^O - } \right){/tex} into the molecule is called acetylation of amines.


  2. The reaction of primary and secondary amines with benzenesulphonyl chloride (C6H5SO2Cl, known as Hinsberg's reagent to form sulphonamides is known as Hinsberg's method (or reaction). This method (or reaction) is used for separating 1o, 2o and 3o amines.
    1. The reaction of benzenesulphonyl chloride with primary amine yields N-ethylbenzenesulphonamide.

      The hydrogen attached to N-atom in sulphonamide is strogly acidic due to the presence of strong electron withdrawing sulphonyl group. Hence, it is soluble in alkali.
    2. In the reaction with secondary amine, N, N-diethylbenzenes amide is formed.

      Since, N, N-diethylbenzenesulphonamide does not contain any hydrogen atom attached to nitrogen atom, it is not acidic and hence, insoluble in alkali.
    3. Tertiary amines do not react with benzenesulphonyl chloride. as it doesn't contain replaceable hydrogens.

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
Give one chemical test to distinguish between the following pairs of compounds:

  1. Methylamine and dimethylamine 
  2. Secondary and tertiary amines 
  3. Ethylamine and aniline 
  4. Aniline and benzylamine 
  5. Aniline and N-methyl aniline
question_text: Give one chemical test to distinguish between the following pairs of compounds: Methylamine and dimethylamine Secondary and tertiary amines Ethylamine and aniline Aniline and benzylamine Aniline and N-methyl aniline
explanation: These can be distinguished by the carbylamine test. {tex}\mathop {C{H_3}N{H_2} + }\limits_{\mathop {Methyla\min e}\limits_{({1^0}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} A\min e)} } CHC{l_3} + \mathop {3KOH}\limits_{(alc)} \xrightarrow{\Delta }\mathop {C{H_3}NC + 3KCl + 3{H_2}O}\limits_{\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {Methylisocyanide} \\ {(offensive{\kern 1pt} smell)} \end{array}} {/tex} {tex}\mathop {C{H_3}NH}\limits_{\mathop {Dimethyla\min e}\limits_{({2^0})\,a\min e)} } \,\xrightarrow{{CHC{l_3}/KOH(alc)}}No\,reaction{/tex} ​ Secondary and tertiary amines can be distinguished by Libermann nitrosoamine test. 2° amines react with nitrous acid to form N-nitrosamines while 3° do not. {tex}\mathop {{{(C{H_3}C{H_2})}_2}}\limits_{Diethyla\min e} NH + HO - N = 0 \to \mathop {(C{H_3}CH_2)_2N - N = O + {H_2}O}\limits_{\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {N - Nitrosodiethyla\min e} \\ {(Yellow\,\,colour)} \end{array}} {/tex} Azo dye test: Aniline and ethylamine can be distinguished by azo dye test. It involves the reaction of aniline with HNO 2 at 273-278 K followed by treatment with an alkaline solution of β-naphthol, which gives a brilliant yellow, orange or red coloured dye. Ethylamine under these condition gives a brisk evolution of N 2 gas with the formation of primary alcohol. Nitrous acid test: Benzylamine reacts with nitrous acid to form a diazonium salt which being unstable even at low temperature, decomposes with evolution of N 2 gas. Aniline, on the other hand, reacts with nitrous acid to form benzene diazonium chloride which is stable at 273 - 278 K and hence does not decompose to evolve N 2 gas. Carbylamine test: Aniline being a primary amine gives carbylamine test whereas N-methylamine being a secondary amine does not give this test. when aniline is heated with an alcoholic solution of KOH and CHCl 3, it gives the offensive smell of phenyl isocyanide.
solution: These can be distinguished by the carbylamine test. {tex}\mathop {C{H_3}N{H_2} + }\limits_{\mathop {Methyla\min e}\limits_{({1^0}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} A\min e)} } CHC{l_3} + \mathop {3KOH}\limits_{(alc)} \xrightarrow{\Delta }\mathop {C{H_3}NC + 3KCl + 3{H_2}O}\limits_{\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {Methylisocyanide} \\ {(offensive{\kern 1pt} smell)} \end{array}} {/tex} {tex}\mathop {C{H_3}NH}\limits_{\mathop {Dimethyla\min e}\limits_{({2^0})\,a\min e)} } \,\xrightarrow{{CHC{l_3}/KOH(alc)}}No\,reaction{/tex} ​ Secondary and tertiary amines can be distinguished by Libermann nitrosoamine test. 2° amines react with nitrous acid to form N-nitrosamines while 3° do not. {tex}\mathop {{{(C{H_3}C{H_2})}_2}}\limits_{Diethyla\min e} NH + HO - N = 0 \to \mathop {(C{H_3}CH_2)_2N - N = O + {H_2}O}\limits_{\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {N - Nitrosodiethyla\min e} \\ {(Yellow\,\,colour)} \end{array}} {/tex} Azo dye test: Aniline and ethylamine can be distinguished by azo dye test. It involves the reaction of aniline with HNO 2 at 273-278 K followed by treatment with an alkaline solution of β-naphthol, which gives a brilliant yellow, orange or red coloured dye. Ethylamine under these condition gives a brisk evolution of N 2 gas with the formation of primary alcohol. Nitrous acid test: Benzylamine reacts with nitrous acid to form a diazonium salt which being unstable even at low temperature, decomposes with evolution of N 2 gas. Aniline, on the other hand, reacts with nitrous acid to form benzene diazonium chloride which is stable at 273 - 278 K and hence does not decompose to evolve N 2 gas. Carbylamine test: Aniline being a primary amine gives carbylamine test whereas N-methylamine being a secondary amine does not give this test. when aniline is heated with an alcoholic solution of KOH and CHCl 3, it gives the offensive smell of phenyl isocyanide.
solution_html:
1. These can be distinguished by the carbylamine test.
    {tex}\mathop {C{H_3}N{H_2} + }\limits_{\mathop {Methyla\min e}\limits_{({1^0}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} A\min e)} } CHC{l_3} + \mathop {3KOH}\limits_{(alc)} \xrightarrow{\Delta }\mathop {C{H_3}NC + 3KCl + 3{H_2}O}\limits_{\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {Methylisocyanide} \\ {(offensive{\kern 1pt} smell)} \end{array}} {/tex}
    {tex}\mathop {C{H_3}NH}\limits_{\mathop {Dimethyla\min e}\limits_{({2^0})\,a\min e)} } \,\xrightarrow{{CHC{l_3}/KOH(alc)}}No\,reaction{/tex}​
  2. Secondary and tertiary amines can be distinguished by Libermann nitrosoamine test. 2° amines react with nitrous acid to form N-nitrosamines while 3° do not.
    {tex}\mathop {{{(C{H_3}C{H_2})}_2}}\limits_{Diethyla\min e} NH + HO - N = 0 \to \mathop {(C{H_3}CH_2)_2N - N = O + {H_2}O}\limits_{\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {N - Nitrosodiethyla\min e} \\ {(Yellow\,\,colour)} \end{array}} {/tex}
  3. Azo dye test: Aniline and ethylamine can be distinguished by azo dye test. It involves the reaction of aniline with HNO2 at 273-278 K followed by treatment with an alkaline solution of β-naphthol, which gives a brilliant yellow, orange or red coloured dye. Ethylamine under these condition gives a brisk evolution of N2 gas with the formation of primary alcohol.
  4. Nitrous acid test: Benzylamine reacts with nitrous acid to form a diazonium salt which being unstable even at low temperature, decomposes with evolution of N2 gas.
    Aniline, on the other hand, reacts with nitrous acid to form benzene diazonium chloride which is stable at 273 - 278 K and hence does not decompose to evolve N2 gas.
  5. Carbylamine test: Aniline being a primary amine gives carbylamine test whereas N-methylamine being a secondary amine does not give this test. when aniline is heated with an alcoholic solution of KOH and CHCl3, it gives the offensive smell of phenyl isocyanide.

explanation: Among the given compound, C 6 H 5 NH 2 has the highest molecular mass of 93 u followed by (C 2 H 5 ) 2 NH with a molecular mass of 73 u while C 2 H 5 NH 2 has the lowest molecular mass of 45 u. Thus, solubility increases in order in which molecular mass decreases, i.e C 6 H 5 NH 2 < (C 2 H 5 ) 2 NH < C 2 H 5 NH 2 .

subject: Chemistry

CH3NH2 or
question_text: Which of the two is more basic and why? CH 3 NH 2 or
explanation: Methylamine is more basic than aniline because alkyl groups (-CH 3 ) are electron releasing due to their +I-effect. The lone pair of electrons on N-atom in aniline is delocalized due to resonance with a benzene ring and hence less available to accept a proton.
solution: Methylamine is more basic than aniline because alkyl groups (-CH 3 ) are electron releasing due to their +I-effect. The lone pair of electrons on N-atom in aniline is delocalized due to resonance with a benzene ring and hence less available to accept a proton.

(Write the chemical equations involved.)
question_text: How will you convert the following? Nitrobenzene into aniline. Ethanoic acid into methanamine. Aniline into N-phenylethanamide. (Write the chemical equations involved.)
explanation: Nitrobenzene into aniline {tex}\mathop {{C_6}{H_5}N{O_2}}\limits_{Nitrobenzene} {/tex} + 3H 2 {tex}\xrightarrow[{or\,Fe\, + \,HCl}]{{Sn\, + \,HCl}}{/tex} {tex}\mathop {{C_6}{H_5}NH_2}\limits_{Aniline} {/tex} + 2H 2 O Ethanoic acid into methanamine {tex}\mathop {{CH_3COOH}}\limits_{Ethanoic\ acid} {/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow{SOCl_2}{/tex} {tex}\mathop {{CH_3COCl}}\limits_{Ethanoyl\ chloride} {/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow{NH_3(excess)}{/tex} {tex}\mathop {{CH_3CONH_2}}\limits_{Ethanamide} {/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow[Hoffman\ bromamide\ reaction ]{Br_2/KOH}{/tex} {tex}\mathop {{CH_3NH_2}}\limits_{Methanamine} {/tex} Aniline into N-phenylethanamide
solution: Nitrobenzene into aniline {tex}\mathop {{C_6}{H_5}N{O_2}}\limits_{Nitrobenzene} {/tex} + 3H 2 {tex}\xrightarrow[{or\,Fe\, + \,HCl}]{{Sn\, + \,HCl}}{/tex} {tex}\mathop {{C_6}{H_5}NH_2}\limits_{Aniline} {/tex} + 2H 2 O Ethanoic acid into methanamine {tex}\mathop {{CH_3COOH}}\limits_{Ethanoic\ acid} {/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow{SOCl_2}{/tex} {tex}\mathop {{CH_3COCl}}\limits_{Ethanoyl\ chloride} {/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow{NH_3(excess)}{/tex} {tex}\mathop {{CH_3CONH_2}}\limits_{Ethanamide} {/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow[Hoffman\ bromamide\ reaction ]{Br_2/KOH}{/tex} {tex}\mathop {{CH_3NH_2}}\limits_{Methanamine} {/tex} Aniline into N-phenylethanamide
solution_html:
1. Nitrobenzene into aniline
    {tex}\mathop {{C_6}{H_5}N{O_2}}\limits_{Nitrobenzene} {/tex} + 3H2 {tex}\xrightarrow[{or\,Fe\, + \,HCl}]{{Sn\, + \,HCl}}{/tex} {tex}\mathop {{C_6}{H_5}NH_2}\limits_{Aniline} {/tex}  + 2H2O
  2. Ethanoic acid into methanamine 
    {tex}\mathop {{CH_3COOH}}\limits_{Ethanoic\ acid} {/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow{SOCl_2}{/tex} {tex}\mathop {{CH_3COCl}}\limits_{Ethanoyl\ chloride} {/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow{NH_3(excess)}{/tex} {tex}\mathop {{CH_3CONH_2}}\limits_{Ethanamide} {/tex}{tex}\xrightarrow[Hoffman\ bromamide\ reaction ]{Br_2/KOH}{/tex} {tex}\mathop {{CH_3NH_2}}\limits_{Methanamine} {/tex}
  3. Aniline into N-phenylethanamide

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
1. Arrange the following compounds
    1. In increasing order of basic strength: C6H5NH2, C6H5N(CH3)2, (C2H5)2NH and CH3NH2.
    2. In decreasing order of pKb values: C2H5NH2, C6H5NHCH3, (C2H5)2NH and C6H5NH2.
    3. In increasing order of basic strength in water: C6H5NH2, (C2H5)2NH, (C2H5)3N, C2H5NH2.
question_text: Arrange the following compounds In increasing order of basic strength: C 6 H 5 NH 2 , C 6 H 5 N(CH 3 ) 2 , (C 2 H 5 ) 2 NH and CH 3 NH 2 . In decreasing order of pK b values: C 2 H 5 NH 2 , C 6 H 5 NHCH 3 , (C 2 H 5 ) 2 NH and C 6 H 5 NH 2 . In increasing order of basic strength in water: C 6 H 5 NH 2 , (C 2 H 5 ) 2 NH, (C 2 H 5 ) 3 N, C 2 H 5 NH 2 .
explanation: C 6 H 5 NH 2 < C 6 H 5 N(CH 3 ) 2 < CH 3 NH 2 < (C 2 H 5 ) 2 NH least basic due to -I-effect of the aryl group. Due to delocalisation of lone pair of electrons of the N-atom over the benzene ring, electrons are not available for donation. Hence, C 6 H 5 NH 2 and C 6 NH 5 CH 3 is far less basic than C 2 H 5 NH 2 and (C 2 H 5 ) 2 NH. Further, due to +I-effect of the CH 3 group, C 6 H 3 NHCH 3 is a little more basic than C 6 H 5 NH 2 . Among C 2 H 5 NH 2 and (C 2 H 5 ) 2 NH, (C 2 H 5 ) 2 NH is more basic than C 6 H 5 NH 2 due to greater +I-effect of the two -C 2 H 5 groups. Thus, the relative basic strength decreases in the order: (C 2 H 5 ) 2 NH > C 2 H 5 NH 2 > C 6 NH 5 CH 3 > C 6 H 5 NH 2 Weak basic strength means larger pK b value. Hence the order of decreasing pK b value is (C 2 H 5 ) 2 NH < C 2 H 5 NH 2 < C 6 H 5 NHCH 3 < C 6 H 5 NH 2 In C 2 H 5 NH 2 ,(C 2 H 5 )NH and (C 2 H 5 ) 3 N, the +I-effect of C 2 H 5 groups increases the electron density on the N-atom. However, in C 6 H 5 NH 2 , the electron density on the N-atom decreases due to delocalisation of the lone pair of electrons over the benzene ring and are not available for donation. Thus, all three ethyl amines are more basic than C 6 H 5 NH 2 . As, 2 o amines are more stable than 1 o and 3 o amines in aqueous solution because all the 3 factors, H-bonding +I effect and steric effect are favourable for 2 o amine. therefore, {tex}\left(\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5}\right)_{2} \mathrm{NH}{/tex} is more basic than {tex}\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{NH}_{2}{/tex} and {tex}\left(\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5}\right)_{3} \mathrm{N}{/tex} . Since, the stabilisation of the conjugate acid derived from {tex}\left(\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5}\right)_{3} \mathrm{N}{/tex} due to +I-effect is greater than the stabilisation of the conjugate acid derived from C 2 H 5 NH 2 by H-bonding, therefore, it is more basic than C 2 H 5 NH 2 . Hence, the overall basic strength increases as C 6 H 5 NH 2 < C 2 H 5 NH 2 < (C 2 H 5 ) 3 N < (C 2 H 5 )NH.
solution: C 6 H 5 NH 2 < C 6 H 5 N(CH 3 ) 2 < CH 3 NH 2 < (C 2 H 5 ) 2 NH least basic due to -I-effect of the aryl group. Due to delocalisation of lone pair of electrons of the N-atom over the benzene ring, electrons are not available for donation. Hence, C 6 H 5 NH 2 and C 6 NH 5 CH 3 is far less basic than C 2 H 5 NH 2 and (C 2 H 5 ) 2 NH. Further, due to +I-effect of the CH 3 group, C 6 H 3 NHCH 3 is a little more basic than C 6 H 5 NH 2 . Among C 2 H 5 NH 2 and (C 2 H 5 ) 2 NH, (C 2 H 5 ) 2 NH is more basic than C 6 H 5 NH 2 due to greater +I-effect of the two -C 2 H 5 groups. Thus, the relative basic strength decreases in the order: (C 2 H 5 ) 2 NH > C 2 H 5 NH 2 > C 6 NH 5 CH 3 > C 6 H 5 NH 2 Weak basic strength means larger pK b value. Hence the order of decreasing pK b value is (C 2 H 5 ) 2 NH < C 2 H 5 NH 2 < C 6 H 5 NHCH 3 < C 6 H 5 NH 2 In C 2 H 5 NH 2 ,(C 2 H 5 )NH and (C 2 H 5 ) 3 N, the +I-effect of C 2 H 5 groups increases the electron density on the N-atom. However, in C 6 H 5 NH 2 , the electron density on the N-atom decreases due to delocalisation of the lone pair of electrons over the benzene ring and are not available for donation. Thus, all three ethyl amines are more basic than C 6 H 5 NH 2 . As, 2 o amines are more stable than 1 o and 3 o amines in aqueous solution because all the 3 factors, H-bonding +I effect and steric effect are favourable for 2 o amine. therefore, {tex}\left(\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5}\right)_{2} \mathrm{NH}{/tex} is more basic than {tex}\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{NH}_{2}{/tex} and {tex}\left(\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5}\right)_{3} \mathrm{N}{/tex} . Since, the stabilisation of the conjugate acid derived from {tex}\left(\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5}\right)_{3} \mathrm{N}{/tex} due to +I-effect is greater than the stabilisation of the conjugate acid derived from C 2 H 5 NH 2 by H-bonding, therefore, it is more basic than C 2 H 5 NH 2 . Hence, the overall basic strength increases as C 6 H 5 NH 2 < C 2 H 5 NH 2 < (C 2 H 5 ) 3 N < (C 2 H 5 )NH.
solution_html:
1. C6H5NH2 < C6H5N(CH3)2 < CH3NH2 < (C2H5)2NH least basic due to -I-effect of the aryl group.
  2. Due to delocalisation of lone pair of electrons of the N-atom over the benzene ring, electrons are not available for donation. Hence, C6H5NH2 and C6NH5CH3 is far less basic than C2H5NH2 and (C2H5)2NH.
    Further, due to +I-effect of the CH3 group, C6H3NHCH3 is a little more basic than C6H5NH2. Among C2H5NH2 and (C2H5)2NH, (C2H5)2NH is more basic than C6H5NH2 due to greater +I-effect of the two -C2H5 groups.
    Thus, the relative basic strength decreases in the order:
    (C2H5)2NH > C2H5NH2 > C6NH5CH3 > C6H5NH2
    Weak basic strength means larger pKb value. Hence the order of decreasing pKb value is
    (C2H5)2NH < C2H5NH2 < C6H5NHCH3 < C6H5NH2
  3. In C2H5NH2,(C2H5)NH and (C2H5)3N, the +I-effect of C2H5 groups increases the electron density on the N-atom. However, in C6H5NH2, the electron density on the N-atom decreases due to delocalisation of the lone pair of electrons over the benzene ring and are not available for donation. Thus, all three ethyl amines are more basic than C6H5NH2.
    As, 2o amines are more stable than 1o and 3o amines in aqueous solution because all the 3 factors, H-bonding +I effect and steric effect are favourable for 2o amine. therefore, {tex}\left(\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5}\right)_{2} \mathrm{NH}{/tex} is more basic than {tex}\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{NH}_{2}{/tex} and {tex}\left(\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5}\right)_{3} \mathrm{N}{/tex}. Since, the stabilisation of the conjugate acid derived from {tex}\left(\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5}\right)_{3} \mathrm{N}{/tex} due to +I-effect is greater than the stabilisation of the conjugate acid derived from C2H5NH2 by H-bonding, therefore, it is more basic than C2H5NH2.
    Hence, the overall basic strength increases as C6H5NH2 < C2H5NH2 < (C2H5)3N < (C2H5)NH.

CH3NH2, (CH3)3N,(CH3)2NH.

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
Give reasons:

  1. Aniline is a weaker base than cyclohexyl amine.
  2. It is difficult to prepare pure amines by ammonolysis of alkyl halides.
question_text: Give reasons: Aniline is a weaker base than cyclohexyl amine. It is difficult to prepare pure amines by ammonolysis of alkyl halides.
explanation: In aniline, the lone pair present on nitrogen is involved in delocalisation. Thus, electrons are not available for donation. There is no such delocalisation in cyclohexylamine. Hence, aniline is a weaker base than cyclohexylamine. It is difficult to prepare pure amines by ammonolysis of alkyl halides because it is nucleophilic substitution reaction and ammonia behave as a nucleophile to form 1 o amine. Now, this 1 o amine behave as a {tex}\mathrm{Nu}^{\ominus}{/tex} and reacts with an alkyl halide to give 2 o amine, which on further reaction yields 3 o amine and quaternary ammonium salt.
solution: In aniline, the lone pair present on nitrogen is involved in delocalisation. Thus, electrons are not available for donation. There is no such delocalisation in cyclohexylamine. Hence, aniline is a weaker base than cyclohexylamine. It is difficult to prepare pure amines by ammonolysis of alkyl halides because it is nucleophilic substitution reaction and ammonia behave as a nucleophile to form 1 o amine. Now, this 1 o amine behave as a {tex}\mathrm{Nu}^{\ominus}{/tex} and reacts with an alkyl halide to give 2 o amine, which on further reaction yields 3 o amine and quaternary ammonium salt.
solution_html:
1. In aniline, the lone pair present on nitrogen is involved in delocalisation. Thus, electrons are not available for donation. There is no such delocalisation in cyclohexylamine. Hence, aniline is a weaker base than cyclohexylamine. 
  2. It is difficult to prepare pure amines by ammonolysis of alkyl halides because it is nucleophilic substitution reaction and ammonia behave as a nucleophile to form 1o amine.
    Now, this 1o amine behave as a {tex}\mathrm{Nu}^{\ominus}{/tex} and reacts with an alkyl halide to give 2o amine, which on further reaction yields 3o amine and quaternary ammonium salt.

question_text: Complete the following reaction: CH 3 CH 2 NH 2 + CHCl 3 + alc. KOH {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{N}_{2}^{+} \mathrm{Cl^-}{/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow[(At\ room\ temperature)]{H_2O}{/tex}
explanation: Carbylamine reaction, i.e. conversion of -NH 2 group in -NC group. {tex}\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{NH}_{2}+\mathrm{CHCl}_{3}+3 \mathrm{KOH}(\mathrm{alc} .){/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow{\Delta}{/tex} {tex}\begin{array}{l}{\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2}-\mathrm{N} \mathop = \limits^ \to \mathrm{C}} \\ {\text { Ethyl isocyanide }}\end{array}{/tex} + 3KCl + 3H 2 O Benzene diazonium chloride form phenol with the reaction of water molecules and N 2 is liberated. {tex}\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{N}_{2}^{+} \mathrm{Cl}^{-}{/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow[298K]{H_2O}{/tex} {tex}\mathop {{C_6}{H_5}OH}\limits_{Phenol} {/tex} + N 2 {tex}\uparrow{/tex} + HCl Dlazonium salts form alcohol with boiling water and acidic medium.
solution: Carbylamine reaction, i.e. conversion of -NH 2 group in -NC group. {tex}\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{NH}_{2}+\mathrm{CHCl}_{3}+3 \mathrm{KOH}(\mathrm{alc} .){/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow{\Delta}{/tex} {tex}\begin{array}{l}{\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2}-\mathrm{N} \mathop = \limits^ \to \mathrm{C}} \\ {\text { Ethyl isocyanide }}\end{array}{/tex} + 3KCl + 3H 2 O Benzene diazonium chloride form phenol with the reaction of water molecules and N 2 is liberated. {tex}\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{N}_{2}^{+} \mathrm{Cl}^{-}{/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow[298K]{H_2O}{/tex} {tex}\mathop {{C_6}{H_5}OH}\limits_{Phenol} {/tex} + N 2 {tex}\uparrow{/tex} + HCl Dlazonium salts form alcohol with boiling water and acidic medium.
solution_html:
1. Carbylamine reaction, i.e. conversion of -NH2 group in -NC group.
    {tex}\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{NH}_{2}+\mathrm{CHCl}_{3}+3 \mathrm{KOH}(\mathrm{alc} .){/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow{\Delta}{/tex} {tex}\begin{array}{l}{\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2}-\mathrm{N} \mathop = \limits^ \to \mathrm{C}} \\ {\text { Ethyl isocyanide }}\end{array}{/tex} + 3KCl + 3H2O
  2. Benzene diazonium chloride form phenol with the reaction of water molecules and N2 is liberated.
    {tex}\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{N}_{2}^{+} \mathrm{Cl}^{-}{/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow[298K]{H_2O}{/tex} {tex}\mathop {{C_6}{H_5}OH}\limits_{Phenol} {/tex} + N2{tex}\uparrow{/tex} + HCl
  3. Dlazonium salts form alcohol with boiling water and acidic medium.

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
Write short notes on the following:

  1. Carbylamine reaction
  2. Diazotisation
  3. Hofmann's bromamide reaction
  4. Coupling reaction
  5. Ammonolysis
question_text: Write short notes on the following: Carbylamine reaction Diazotisation Hofmann's bromamide reaction Coupling reaction Ammonolysis
explanation: Carbylamine reaction: Carbylamine reaction is used as a test for the identification of primary amines. When aliphatic and aromatic primary amines are heated with chloroform and ethanolic potassium hydroxide, carbylamines (or isocyanides) are formed. These carbylamines have very unpleasant odours. Secondary and tertiary amines do not respond to this test. For example, {tex}\mathop {C{H_3} - N{H_2}}\limits_{Methana\min e} \; + \;CHC{l_3}\; + \;3KOH(alc.)\;\xrightarrow{\Delta }\mathop {\,C{H_3} - NC}\limits_{Methyl\;carbyla\min e} \; + \;3KCl\; + \;3{H_2}O{/tex} Diazotisation: Aromatic primary amines react with nitrous acid (prepared in situ from NaNO 2 and HCl) at 273 - 278 K to form diazonium salts. This conversion of aromatic primary amines into diazonium salts is known as diazotization. For example, Hoffmann bromamide reaction: When an amide is treated with bromine in an aqueous or ethanolic solution of sodium hydroxide, a primary amine with one carbon atom less than the original amide is produced. This degradation reaction is known as Hoffmann bromamide reaction. This reaction involves the migration of an alkyl or aryl group from the carbonyl carbon atom of the amide to the nitrogen atom. For example, {tex}\mathop {C{H_3} - \mathop {\mathop C\limits^{||} }\limits^O - N{H_2}}\limits_{Ethanamide} \; + \;B{r_2}\; + \;4NaOH \to \mathop {C{H_3} - N{H_2}}\limits_{Methana\min e} \; + \;N{a_2}C{O_3}\; + 2NaBr\; + \;2{H_2}O{/tex} {tex}\mathop {{C_6}{H_5} - \mathop {\mathop C\limits^{||} }\limits^O - N{H_2}}\limits_{Benzamide} \; + \;B{r_2}\; + \;4NaOH \to \mathop {{C_6}{H_5} - N{H_2}}\limits_{Aniline} \; + \;N{a_2}C{O_3}\; + 2NaBr\; + \;2{H_2}O{/tex} Coupling reaction: The reaction of joining two aromatic rings through the - N = N - bond is known as coupling reaction. Benzene diazonium salt reacts with phenol or aromatic amines to form coloured azo compounds. Ammonolysis: When an alkyl or benzyl halide is allowed to react with an ethanolic solution of ammonia, it undergoes nucleophilic substitution reaction in which the halogen atom is replaced by an amino {tex}\left( { - N{H_2}} \right){/tex} (-NH 2 ) group. This process of cleavage of the carbon-halogen bond is known as ammonolysis. When this substituted ammonium salt is treated with a strong base such as sodium hydroxide, amine is obtained. {tex}R - \mathop N\limits^ + {H_3}\mathop X\limits^ - \; + \;NaOH \to \mathop {R - N{H_2}}\limits_{A\min e} + \;{H_2}O\; + \;NaX{/tex} Though primary amine is produced as the major product, this process produces a mixture of primary, secondary and tertiary amines, and also a quaternary ammonium salt as shown. {tex}\mathop {RN{H_2}}\limits_{({1^ \circ })} \xrightarrow{{RX}}\mathop {{R_2}NH}\limits_{({2^ \circ })} \xrightarrow{{RX}}\mathop {{R_3}N}\limits_{({3^ \circ })} \xrightarrow{{RX}}\mathop {{R_4}\mathop N\limits^ + \mathop X\limits^ - }\limits_{Quaternary\;ammonium\;salt} {/tex}
solution: Carbylamine reaction: Carbylamine reaction is used as a test for the identification of primary amines. When aliphatic and aromatic primary amines are heated with chloroform and ethanolic potassium hydroxide, carbylamines (or isocyanides) are formed. These carbylamines have very unpleasant odours. Secondary and tertiary amines do not respond to this test. For example, {tex}\mathop {C{H_3} - N{H_2}}\limits_{Methana\min e} \; + \;CHC{l_3}\; + \;3KOH(alc.)\;\xrightarrow{\Delta }\mathop {\,C{H_3} - NC}\limits_{Methyl\;carbyla\min e} \; + \;3KCl\; + \;3{H_2}O{/tex} Diazotisation: Aromatic primary amines react with nitrous acid (prepared in situ from NaNO 2 and HCl) at 273 - 278 K to form diazonium salts. This conversion of aromatic primary amines into diazonium salts is known as diazotization. For example, Hoffmann bromamide reaction: When an amide is treated with bromine in an aqueous or ethanolic solution of sodium hydroxide, a primary amine with one carbon atom less than the original amide is produced. This degradation reaction is known as Hoffmann bromamide reaction. This reaction involves the migration of an alkyl or aryl group from the carbonyl carbon atom of the amide to the nitrogen atom. For example, {tex}\mathop {C{H_3} - \mathop {\mathop C\limits^{||} }\limits^O - N{H_2}}\limits_{Ethanamide} \; + \;B{r_2}\; + \;4NaOH \to \mathop {C{H_3} - N{H_2}}\limits_{Methana\min e} \; + \;N{a_2}C{O_3}\; + 2NaBr\; + \;2{H_2}O{/tex} {tex}\mathop {{C_6}{H_5} - \mathop {\mathop C\limits^{||} }\limits^O - N{H_2}}\limits_{Benzamide} \; + \;B{r_2}\; + \;4NaOH \to \mathop {{C_6}{H_5} - N{H_2}}\limits_{Aniline} \; + \;N{a_2}C{O_3}\; + 2NaBr\; + \;2{H_2}O{/tex} Coupling reaction: The reaction of joining two aromatic rings through the - N = N - bond is known as coupling reaction. Benzene diazonium salt reacts with phenol or aromatic amines to form coloured azo compounds. Ammonolysis: When an alkyl or benzyl halide is allowed to react with an ethanolic solution of ammonia, it undergoes nucleophilic substitution reaction in which the halogen atom is replaced by an amino {tex}\left( { - N{H_2}} \right){/tex} (-NH 2 ) group. This process of cleavage of the carbon-halogen bond is known as ammonolysis. When this substituted ammonium salt is treated with a strong base such as sodium hydroxide, amine is obtained. {tex}R - \mathop N\limits^ + {H_3}\mathop X\limits^ - \; + \;NaOH \to \mathop {R - N{H_2}}\limits_{A\min e} + \;{H_2}O\; + \;NaX{/tex} Though primary amine is produced as the major product, this process produces a mixture of primary, secondary and tertiary amines, and also a quaternary ammonium salt as shown. {tex}\mathop {RN{H_2}}\limits_{({1^ \circ })} \xrightarrow{{RX}}\mathop {{R_2}NH}\limits_{({2^ \circ })} \xrightarrow{{RX}}\mathop {{R_3}N}\limits_{({3^ \circ })} \xrightarrow{{RX}}\mathop {{R_4}\mathop N\limits^ + \mathop X\limits^ - }\limits_{Quaternary\;ammonium\;salt} {/tex}
solution_html:
1. Carbylamine reaction: Carbylamine reaction is used as a test for the identification of primary amines. When aliphatic and aromatic primary amines are heated with chloroform and ethanolic potassium hydroxide, carbylamines (or isocyanides) are formed. These carbylamines have very unpleasant odours. Secondary and tertiary amines do not respond to this test. For example,
    {tex}\mathop {C{H_3} - N{H_2}}\limits_{Methana\min e} \; + \;CHC{l_3}\; + \;3KOH(alc.)\;\xrightarrow{\Delta }\mathop {\,C{H_3} - NC}\limits_{Methyl\;carbyla\min e} \; + \;3KCl\; + \;3{H_2}O{/tex}
  2. Diazotisation: Aromatic primary amines react with nitrous acid (prepared in situ from NaNO2 and HCl) at 273 - 278 K to form diazonium salts. This conversion of aromatic primary amines into diazonium salts is known as diazotization. For example,
  3. Hoffmann bromamide reaction: When an amide is treated with bromine in an aqueous or ethanolic solution of sodium hydroxide, a primary amine with one carbon atom less than the original amide is produced. This degradation reaction is known as Hoffmann bromamide reaction. This reaction involves the migration of an alkyl or aryl group from the carbonyl carbon atom of the amide to the nitrogen atom. For example,
    {tex}\mathop {C{H_3} - \mathop {\mathop C\limits^{||} }\limits^O - N{H_2}}\limits_{Ethanamide} \; + \;B{r_2}\; + \;4NaOH \to \mathop {C{H_3} - N{H_2}}\limits_{Methana\min e} \; + \;N{a_2}C{O_3}\; + 2NaBr\; + \;2{H_2}O{/tex}
    {tex}\mathop {{C_6}{H_5} - \mathop {\mathop C\limits^{||} }\limits^O - N{H_2}}\limits_{Benzamide} \; + \;B{r_2}\; + \;4NaOH \to \mathop {{C_6}{H_5} - N{H_2}}\limits_{Aniline} \; + \;N{a_2}C{O_3}\; + 2NaBr\; + \;2{H_2}O{/tex}
     
  4. Coupling reaction: The reaction of joining two aromatic rings through the - N = N - bond is known as coupling reaction. Benzene diazonium salt reacts with phenol or aromatic amines to form coloured azo compounds.
  5. Ammonolysis: When an alkyl or benzyl halide is allowed to react with an ethanolic solution of ammonia, it undergoes nucleophilic substitution reaction in which the halogen atom is replaced by an amino {tex}\left( { - N{H_2}} \right){/tex} (-NH2) group. This process of cleavage of the carbon-halogen bond is known as ammonolysis.

    When this substituted ammonium salt is treated with a strong base such as sodium hydroxide, amine is obtained.
    {tex}R - \mathop N\limits^ + {H_3}\mathop X\limits^ - \; + \;NaOH \to \mathop {R - N{H_2}}\limits_{A\min e} + \;{H_2}O\; + \;NaX{/tex}
    Though primary amine is produced as the major product, this process produces a mixture of primary, secondary and tertiary amines, and also a quaternary ammonium salt as shown.
    {tex}\mathop {RN{H_2}}\limits_{({1^ \circ })} \xrightarrow{{RX}}\mathop {{R_2}NH}\limits_{({2^ \circ })} \xrightarrow{{RX}}\mathop {{R_3}N}\limits_{({3^ \circ })} \xrightarrow{{RX}}\mathop {{R_4}\mathop N\limits^ + \mathop X\limits^ - }\limits_{Quaternary\;ammonium\;salt} {/tex}

explanation: Benzenediazonium chloride is a very reactive compound which oxidises hypophosphorous acid to hypophosphoric acid and the reactant is reduced to benzene. {tex}\begin{array}{*{20}{l}} {{C_6}{H_5}{{\mathop N }_2}\mathop C l} \end{array} + {H_3}P{O_2} + {H_2}O{/tex} {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} {tex}\begin{array}{c}{\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{6}} \\ {\text { Benzene }}\end{array}{/tex} + {tex}\mathrm{N}_{2} \uparrow{/tex} + H 3 PO 3 + HCl

subject: Chemistry
question_html: Primary amines have higher boiling points than tertiary amines. Why?
question_text: Primary amines have higher boiling points than tertiary amines. Why?
explanation: Due to the presence of two H-atoms with N-atom of primary amines, they undergo extensive intermolecular H-bonding while tertiary amines due to the absence of H-atom with the N-atom, do not undergo H- bonding. As a result, primary amines have higher boiling points than tertiary amines.
solution: Due to the presence of two H-atoms with N-atom of primary amines, they undergo extensive intermolecular H-bonding while tertiary amines due to the absence of H-atom with the N-atom, do not undergo H- bonding. As a result, primary amines have higher boiling points than tertiary amines.
solution_html: Due to the presence of two H-atoms with N-atom of primary amines, they undergo extensive intermolecular H-bonding while tertiary amines due to the absence of H-atom with the N-atom, do not undergo H- bonding. As a result, primary amines have higher boiling points than tertiary amines.

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
Arrange the following compounds in a decreasing order of basic strength in their aqueous solutions.
NH3, CH3NH2, (CH3)2NH, (CH3)3N
question_text: Arrange the following compounds in a decreasing order of basic strength in their aqueous solutions. NH 3 , CH 3 NH 2 , (CH 3 ) 2 NH, (CH 3 ) 3 N
explanation: The relative strength of CH 3 NH 2 , (CH 3 ) 2 NH and (CH 3 ) 3 N depends upon the stabilisation of their conjugate acids which depend on the number of factors like H-bonding, steric hindrance and +I-effect of the alkyl groups. All these factors are favourable for 2 o amines, therefore (CH 3 ) 2 NH is a stronger base than CH 3 NH 2 and (CH 3 ) 3 N. Since, {tex}\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right)_{3} \mathrm{N}{/tex} does not have H-atom linked to N-atom, therefore, the stabilisation of the conjugate acid-derived from CH 3 NH 2 due to H-bonding is greater than that of the conjugate acid derived from {tex}\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right)_{3} \mathrm{N}{/tex} . is more stable than . Further, in NH 3 no +I- group is linked to N-atom thus, it is least basic among all the given amines. Hence, the order would be: {tex}\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right)_{2} \mathrm{NH}>\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{NH}_{2}>\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right)_{3} \mathrm{N}>\mathrm{NH}_{3}{/tex} .
solution: The relative strength of CH 3 NH 2 , (CH 3 ) 2 NH and (CH 3 ) 3 N depends upon the stabilisation of their conjugate acids which depend on the number of factors like H-bonding, steric hindrance and +I-effect of the alkyl groups. All these factors are favourable for 2 o amines, therefore (CH 3 ) 2 NH is a stronger base than CH 3 NH 2 and (CH 3 ) 3 N. Since, {tex}\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right)_{3} \mathrm{N}{/tex} does not have H-atom linked to N-atom, therefore, the stabilisation of the conjugate acid-derived from CH 3 NH 2 due to H-bonding is greater than that of the conjugate acid derived from {tex}\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right)_{3} \mathrm{N}{/tex} . is more stable than . Further, in NH 3 no +I- group is linked to N-atom thus, it is least basic among all the given amines. Hence, the order would be: {tex}\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right)_{2} \mathrm{NH}>\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{NH}_{2}>\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right)_{3} \mathrm{N}>\mathrm{NH}_{3}{/tex} .
solution_html:
The relative strength of CH3NH2, (CH3)2NH and (CH3)3N depends upon the stabilisation of their conjugate acids which depend on the number of factors like H-bonding, steric hindrance and +I-effect of the alkyl groups. All these factors are favourable for 2o amines, therefore (CH3)2NH is a stronger base than CH3NH2 and (CH3)3N. Since, {tex}\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right)_{3} \mathrm{N}{/tex} does not have H-atom linked to N-atom, therefore, the stabilisation of the conjugate acid-derived from CH3NH2 due to H-bonding is greater than that of the conjugate acid derived from {tex}\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right)_{3} \mathrm{N}{/tex}.
 is more stable than .
Further, in NH3 no +I- group is linked to N-atom thus, it is least basic among all the given amines. Hence, the order would be:
{tex}\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right)_{2} \mathrm{NH}>\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{NH}_{2}>\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right)_{3} \mathrm{N}>\mathrm{NH}_{3}{/tex}.

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
1. Why aniline does not undergo Friedel-Crafts reaction?
  2. Why can primary aromatic amines be not prepared by Gabriel phthalimide synthesis?
question_text: Why aniline does not undergo Friedel-Crafts reaction? Why can primary aromatic amines be not prepared by Gabriel phthalimide synthesis?
explanation: Aniline being a Lewis base reacts with Lewis acid (AlCl 3 )to form a salt. {tex}\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{NH}_{2}+\mathrm{AlCl}_{3}{/tex} {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} {tex}{C_6}{H_5}\mathop N\limits^+{H_2}AlCl_3^ - {/tex} As a result, N acquires a positive charge so, it acts as a strong deactivating group for electrophilic substitution reaction. Thus, aniline does not undergo Friedel-Crafts reaction. Primary aromatic amines cannot be prepared by Gabriel phthalimide synthesis because aryl halides do not undergo nucleophilic substitution with the anion formed by phthalimide.
solution: Aniline being a Lewis base reacts with Lewis acid (AlCl 3 )to form a salt. {tex}\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{NH}_{2}+\mathrm{AlCl}_{3}{/tex} {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} {tex}{C_6}{H_5}\mathop N\limits^+{H_2}AlCl_3^ - {/tex} As a result, N acquires a positive charge so, it acts as a strong deactivating group for electrophilic substitution reaction. Thus, aniline does not undergo Friedel-Crafts reaction. Primary aromatic amines cannot be prepared by Gabriel phthalimide synthesis because aryl halides do not undergo nucleophilic substitution with the anion formed by phthalimide.
solution_html:
1. Aniline being a Lewis base reacts with Lewis acid (AlCl3)to form a salt.
    {tex}\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{NH}_{2}+\mathrm{AlCl}_{3}{/tex} {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} {tex}{C_6}{H_5}\mathop N\limits^+{H_2}AlCl_3^ - {/tex}
    As a result, N acquires a positive charge so, it acts as a strong deactivating group for electrophilic substitution reaction. Thus, aniline does not undergo Friedel-Crafts reaction.
  2. Primary aromatic amines cannot be prepared by Gabriel phthalimide synthesis because aryl halides do not undergo nucleophilic substitution with the anion formed by phthalimide.

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
Account for the following:

  1. Diazonium salts of aromatic amines are more stable than those of aliphatic amines.
  2. Amines are more basic than alcohols of comparable molecular masses.
question_text: Account for the following: Diazonium salts of aromatic amines are more stable than those of aliphatic amines. Amines are more basic than alcohols of comparable molecular masses.
explanation: The diazonium salts of aromatic amines are more stable than those of aliphatic amines due to dispersal of the positive charge on the benzene ring as shown below. Amines are more basic than alcohols of comparable masses because N-atom being less electronegative can easily donate its lone pair of electrons to a proton than more electronegative 0-atom which hold electrons more tightly than N. Hence, amines are more basic than alcohols. When an amine accepts a proton, the ammonium salt is formed and when alcohol accepts a proton oxonium salt is formed. Since N being less electronegative can accommodate the positive charge better than the more electronegative O-atom, therefore, ammonium salts are more stable than oxonium salts. As a result, amines are more basic than alcohols.
solution: The diazonium salts of aromatic amines are more stable than those of aliphatic amines due to dispersal of the positive charge on the benzene ring as shown below. Amines are more basic than alcohols of comparable masses because N-atom being less electronegative can easily donate its lone pair of electrons to a proton than more electronegative 0-atom which hold electrons more tightly than N. Hence, amines are more basic than alcohols. When an amine accepts a proton, the ammonium salt is formed and when alcohol accepts a proton oxonium salt is formed. Since N being less electronegative can accommodate the positive charge better than the more electronegative O-atom, therefore, ammonium salts are more stable than oxonium salts. As a result, amines are more basic than alcohols.
solution_html:
1. The diazonium salts of aromatic amines are more stable than those of aliphatic amines due to dispersal of the positive charge on the benzene ring as shown below.
  2. Amines are more basic than alcohols of comparable masses because
    1. N-atom being less electronegative can easily donate its lone pair of electrons to a proton than more electronegative 0-atom which hold electrons more tightly than N. Hence, amines are more basic than alcohols.
    2. When an amine accepts a proton, the ammonium salt is formed and when alcohol accepts a proton oxonium salt is formed.

    3. Since N being less electronegative can accommodate the positive charge better than the more electronegative O-atom, therefore, ammonium salts are more stable than oxonium salts. As a result, amines are more basic than alcohols.

question_text: Give a chemical test to distinguish between methylamine and dimethylamine. aniline and N-methylaniline.
explanation: Methylamine being a primary amine gives carbylamine test, i.e. on treatment with alc. KOH and chloroform, followed by heating, it gives offensive odour of methyl isocyanide. Dimethylamine being a secondary amine does not give this test. Aniline is a primary aromatic amine while N-methylaniline is a secondary aromatic amine. Both can be distinguished by carbylamine reaction. On adding CHCl 3 and KOH, aniline will give offensive smell due to the formation of phenyl carbylamine (phenyl isocyanide), while N-methylariiline will not react.

question_html:
1. Write one chemical reaction for each
    1. Carbylamine reaction
    2. Acetylation reaction
  2. Write structure of N,N-ethylmethylethanamide
question_text: Write one chemical reaction for each Carbylamine reaction Acetylation reaction Write structure of N,N-ethylmethylethanamide
explanation: Carbylamine reaction: Aliphatic or aromatic primary amines on heating with chloroform and ethanolic potassium hydroxide form isocyanides or carbylamine which are foul smelling substances. This reaction is known as carbylamine reaction. {tex}\begin{array}{c}{\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{NH}_{2}} \\ {\text { Aniline }}\end{array}{/tex} + CHCl 3 + 3KOH {tex}\xrightarrow{\Delta}{/tex} C 6 H 5 NC + 3KCl + 3H 2 O This reaction is used as a test for primary aliphatic and aromatic amine. Acetylation: The structure of N,N-ethylmethylethanamide is: {tex}{C_2}{H_5} - \mathop {\mathop {\mathop N\limits^{.\,.} }\limits_| }\limits_{C{H_3}} - \mathop {\mathop C\limits_{||} }\limits_O - C{H_3}{/tex}
solution: Carbylamine reaction: Aliphatic or aromatic primary amines on heating with chloroform and ethanolic potassium hydroxide form isocyanides or carbylamine which are foul smelling substances. This reaction is known as carbylamine reaction. {tex}\begin{array}{c}{\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{NH}_{2}} \\ {\text { Aniline }}\end{array}{/tex} + CHCl 3 + 3KOH {tex}\xrightarrow{\Delta}{/tex} C 6 H 5 NC + 3KCl + 3H 2 O This reaction is used as a test for primary aliphatic and aromatic amine. 1. Carbylamine reaction: Aliphatic or aromatic primary amines on heating with chloroform and ethanolic potassium hydroxide form isocyanides or carbylamine which are foul smelling substances. This reaction is known as carbylamine reaction.
    {tex}\begin{array}{c}{\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{NH}_{2}} \\ {\text { Aniline }}\end{array}{/tex} + CHCl3 + 3KOH {tex}\xrightarrow{\Delta}{/tex} C6H5NC + 3KCl + 3H2O
    This reaction is used as a test for primary aliphatic and aromatic amine.

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
Giving an example for each describe the following reactions :

  1. Hofmann bromamide reaction.
  2. Gattermann reaction.
  3. Coupling reaction.
question_text: Giving an example for each describe the following reactions : Hofmann bromamide reaction. Gattermann reaction. Coupling reaction.
explanation: Hofmann bromamide degradation reaction It is a method for the preparation of primary amines by treating an amide with bromine in an aqueous or ethanolic solution of sodium hydroxide. The amines so formed contain one carbon less than that present in the parent amide. Gattermann reaction When benzene diazonium chloride is treated with Cu/HCl Cu/HBr, chlorobenzene or bromobenzene is obtained. This reaction is known as Gattermann reaction Coupllng reaction Arenediazonium salts react with highly reactive (i.e. electron rich) aromatic compounds such as aniline, phenols to form brightly coloured azo compounds, Ar-N =N-Ar. This reaction is called coupling reaction. e.g. Benzene diazonium chloride reacts with aniline in faintly acidic medium (pH 4- 5) at 273·278K, in which the molecule at its para-position is coupled with the diazonium salt to form p-aminoazobenzene. This is an example of coupling reaction.
solution: Hofmann bromamide degradation reaction It is a method for the preparation of primary amines by treating an amide with bromine in an aqueous or ethanolic solution of sodium hydroxide. The amines so formed contain one carbon less than that present in the parent amide. Gattermann reaction When benzene diazonium chloride is treated with Cu/HCl Cu/HBr, chlorobenzene or bromobenzene is obtained. This reaction is known as Gattermann reaction Coupllng reaction Arenediazonium salts react with highly reactive (i.e. electron rich) aromatic compounds such as aniline, phenols to form brightly coloured azo compounds, Ar-N =N-Ar. This reaction is called coupling reaction. e.g. Benzene diazonium chloride reacts with aniline in faintly acidic medium (pH 4- 5) at 273·278K, in which the molecule at its para-position is coupled with the diazonium salt to form p-aminoazobenzene. This is an example of coupling reaction.
solution_html:
1. Hofmann bromamide degradation reaction It is a method for the preparation of primary amines by treating an amide with bromine in an aqueous or ethanolic solution of sodium hydroxide. The amines so formed contain one carbon less than that present in the parent amide.
  2. Gattermann reaction When benzene diazonium chloride is treated with Cu/HCl Cu/HBr, chlorobenzene or bromobenzene is obtained. This reaction is known as Gattermann reaction
  3. Coupllng reaction Arenediazonium salts react with highly reactive (i.e. electron rich) aromatic compounds such as aniline, phenols to form brightly coloured azo compounds, Ar-N =N-Ar. This reaction is called coupling reaction. e.g. Benzene diazonium chloride reacts with aniline in faintly acidic medium (pH 4- 5) at 273·278K, in which the molecule at its para-position is coupled with the diazonium salt to form p-aminoazobenzene. This is an example of coupling reaction.

  1. Aniline to nitrobenzene.

question_text: How are the following conversions carried? Aniline to nitrobenzene. Ethanamine to N-ethylethanamide.
explanation: {tex}\begin{array}{c}{\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{NH}_{2}} \\ {\text { Ethanamine }}\end{array}{/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow{HNO_2}{/tex} CH 3 CH 2 OOH {tex}\xrightarrow{[O]}{/tex} CH 3 CHOOH {tex}\xrightarrow{C_2H_5NH_2}{/tex} {tex}\begin{array}{c}{\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CONHC}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5}} \\ {\text { N-ethylethanamide }}\end{array}{/tex}
solution: {tex}\begin{array}{c}{\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{NH}_{2}} \\ {\text { Ethanamine }}\end{array}{/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow{HNO_2}{/tex} CH 3 CH 2 OOH {tex}\xrightarrow{[O]}{/tex} CH 3 CHOOH {tex}\xrightarrow{C_2H_5NH_2}{/tex} {tex}\begin{array}{c}{\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CONHC}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5}} \\ {\text { N-ethylethanamide }}\end{array}{/tex}
solution_html: 2. {tex}\begin{array}{c}{\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{NH}_{2}} \\ {\text { Ethanamine }}\end{array}{/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow{HNO_2}{/tex} CH3CH2OOH {tex}\xrightarrow{[O]}{/tex} CH3CHOOH {tex}\xrightarrow{C_2H_5NH_2}{/tex}{tex}\begin{array}{c}{\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CONHC}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5}} \\ {\text { N-ethylethanamide }}\end{array}{/tex}

explanation: {tex}\begin{array}{c}{\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{NH}_{2}} \\ {\text { Ethanamine }}\end{array}{/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow{HNO_2}{/tex} CH 3 CH 2 OOH {tex}\xrightarrow{[O]}{/tex} CH 3 CHOOH {tex}\xrightarrow{C_2H_5NH_2}{/tex} {tex}\begin{array}{c}{\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CONHC}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5}} \\ {\text { N-ethylethanamide }}\end{array}{/tex} {tex}\begin{array}{c}{\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{Cl}} \\ {\text { Chloroethane }}\end{array}{/tex} + KCN {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} {tex}\mathop {{C_2}{H_5}C \equiv N}\limits_{\begin{array}{*{20}{l}} {\begin{array}{*{20}{l}} {\begin{array}{*{20}{l}} {Propane} \end{array}\,nitrile} \end{array}} \end{array}} {/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow[Na/C_2H_5OH]{4[H]}{/tex} {tex}\begin{array}{c}{\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{NH}_{2}} \\ {\text { Propan }-1-\text { amine }}\end{array}{/tex}
solution: {tex}\begin{array}{c}{\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{NH}_{2}} \\ {\text { Ethanamine }}\end{array}{/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow{HNO_2}{/tex} CH 3 CH 2 OOH {tex}\xrightarrow{[O]}{/tex} CH 3 CHOOH {tex}\xrightarrow{C_2H_5NH_2}{/tex} {tex}\begin{array}{c}{\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CONHC}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5}} \\ {\text { N-ethylethanamide }}\end{array}{/tex} {tex}\begin{array}{c}{\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{Cl}} \\ {\text { Chloroethane }}\end{array}{/tex} + KCN {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} {tex}\mathop {{C_2}{H_5}C \equiv N}\limits_{\begin{array}{*{20}{l}} {\begin{array}{*{20}{l}} {\begin{array}{*{20}{l}} {Propane} \end{array}\,nitrile} \end{array}} \end{array}} {/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow[Na/C_2H_5OH]{4[H]}{/tex} {tex}\begin{array}{c}{\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{NH}_{2}} \\ {\text { Propan }-1-\text { amine }}\end{array}{/tex}
solution_html:
2. {tex}\begin{array}{c}{\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{NH}_{2}} \\ {\text { Ethanamine }}\end{array}{/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow{HNO_2}{/tex} CH3CH2OOH {tex}\xrightarrow{[O]}{/tex} CH3CHOOH {tex}\xrightarrow{C_2H_5NH_2}{/tex}{tex}\begin{array}{c}{\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CONHC}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5}} \\ {\text { N-ethylethanamide }}\end{array}{/tex}
  3. {tex}\begin{array}{c}{\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{Cl}} \\ {\text { Chloroethane }}\end{array}{/tex} + KCN {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} {tex}\mathop {{C_2}{H_5}C \equiv N}\limits_{\begin{array}{*{20}{l}} {\begin{array}{*{20}{l}} {\begin{array}{*{20}{l}} {Propane} \end{array}\,nitrile} \end{array}} \end{array}} {/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow[Na/C_2H_5OH]{4[H]}{/tex} {tex}\begin{array}{c}{\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{NH}_{2}} \\ {\text { Propan }-1-\text { amine }}\end{array}{/tex}

question_html:
Complete the following reaction equations:

  1. C6H5NH2 + CH3COCl {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 
  2. C2H5NH2 + C6H5SO2Cl {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 
  3. C2H5NH2 + HNO2 {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex}
question_text: Complete the following reaction equations: C 6 H 5 NH 2 + CH 3 COCl {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} C 2 H 5 NH 2 + C 6 H 5 SO 2 Cl {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} C 2 H 5 NH 2 + HNO 2 {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex}
explanation: C 6 H 5 NH 2 + CH 3 COCl {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} {tex}\begin{array}{c}{\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{NHCOCH}_{3}} \\ {\text { Acetanilide }}\end{array}{/tex} + HCl C 2 H 5 NH 2 + C 6 H 5 SO 2 Cl {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} C 6 H 5 SO 2 NHC 2 H 5 + HCl C 2 H 5 NH 2 + HNO 2 {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} C 2 H 5 OH + N 2 + HCl
solution: C 6 H 5 NH 2 + CH 3 COCl {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} {tex}\begin{array}{c}{\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{NHCOCH}_{3}} \\ {\text { Acetanilide }}\end{array}{/tex} + HCl C 2 H 5 NH 2 + C 6 H 5 SO 2 Cl {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} C 6 H 5 SO 2 NHC 2 H 5 + HCl C 2 H 5 NH 2 + HNO 2 {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} C 2 H 5 OH + N 2 + HCl
solution_html:
1. C6H5NH2 + CH3COCl {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} {tex}\begin{array}{c}{\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{NHCOCH}_{3}} \\ {\text { Acetanilide }}\end{array}{/tex} + HCl
  2. C2H5NH2 + C6H5SO2Cl {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} C6H5SO2NHC2H5 + HCl
  3. C2H5NH2 + HNO2 {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} C2H5OH + N2 + HCl

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
Give reasons:
Methylamine in water reacts with ferric chloride to precipitate ferric hydroxide.
question_text: Give reasons: Methylamine in water reacts with ferric chloride to precipitate ferric hydroxide.
explanation: Methylamine being basic, accepts a proton from water as shown in the equation below. While doing so it liberates a OH - ion which combines with FeCl 3 to form a brown ppt of ferric hydroxide. {tex}{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_3}{\text{N}}{{\text{H}}_2} + {{\text{H}}_2}{\text{O}} \to {\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_3}\mathop {\text{N}}\limits^ + {{\text{H}}_3} + {\text{O}}{{\text{H}}^ - }{/tex} {tex}{\text{FeC}}{{\text{l}}_3} + 3{\text{O}}{{\text{H}}^ - } \to \mathop {{\text{Fe}}{{({\text{OH}})}_3} + 3{\text{C}}{{\text{l}}^ - }}\limits_{(reddish\;brown\;ppt.)} {/tex}
solution: Methylamine being basic, accepts a proton from water as shown in the equation below. While doing so it liberates a OH - ion which combines with FeCl 3 to form a brown ppt of ferric hydroxide. {tex}{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_3}{\text{N}}{{\text{H}}_2} + {{\text{H}}_2}{\text{O}} \to {\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_3}\mathop {\text{N}}\limits^ + {{\text{H}}_3} + {\text{O}}{{\text{H}}^ - }{/tex} {tex}{\text{FeC}}{{\text{l}}_3} + 3{\text{O}}{{\text{H}}^ - } \to \mathop {{\text{Fe}}{{({\text{OH}})}_3} + 3{\text{C}}{{\text{l}}^ - }}\limits_{(reddish\;brown\;ppt.)} {/tex}
solution_html:
Methylamine being basic, accepts a proton from water as shown in the equation below. While doing so it liberates a OH- ion which combines with FeCl3 to form a brown ppt of ferric hydroxide.
{tex}{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_3}{\text{N}}{{\text{H}}_2} + {{\text{H}}_2}{\text{O}} \to {\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_3}\mathop {\text{N}}\limits^ + {{\text{H}}_3} + {\text{O}}{{\text{H}}^ - }{/tex}
{tex}{\text{FeC}}{{\text{l}}_3} + 3{\text{O}}{{\text{H}}^ - } \to \mathop {{\text{Fe}}{{({\text{OH}})}_3} + 3{\text{C}}{{\text{l}}^ - }}\limits_{(reddish\;brown\;ppt.)} {/tex}
